<a id="s2"></a>
## §2. Verses by sub-group

All verses for cluster terms, grouped by the analytical sub-group the term belongs to. Within each sub-group the rows are sorted canonically (book · chapter · verse). The **Term** column shows the Strong's number and transliteration of the cluster term whose `wa_verse_records` row generated this entry; **Spans in verse** lists every term-span recorded at that verse location.

Status precedence: G = group-assigned (analysed) · SA = set-aside · NR = is_relevant=0 · P = pending in VC · UT = untouched (no VC row).

**ID columns:** `vr_id` is `wa_verse_records.id`; `mti_id` is `mti_terms.id` (also `wa_verse_records.mti_term_id` and `verse_context.mti_term_id`). Both are exposed so AI can author VCREVISE / VCNEW / VCVERSE patches directly from this report — no separate ID-resolver query needed. The `(vr_id, mti_id)` pair is the natural key for any `verse_context` operation.

<a id="s3"></a>
## §3. Per-term comprehensive detail

Each term gets numbered sections: identity • meaning • anchor-verse linkages • groups • findings • pointers • auxiliary data. Verses are not repeated here — they are listed by sub-group in §2. Items linked only by registry are summarised here; exhaustive lists are in the appendix §4.

---

<a id="t-H1288"></a>
### H1288 ba.rakh — to bless (`mti_id=1299`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H1288` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R194 blessing · Legacy C-cluster: C16
3. Sub-group: —
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 330
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=1299` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`

**Verses:** 249 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |

Full description restored from wa-vcb-022-term-observations-v2.8-20260404.md |

**Findings — via anchor verse (72 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-043 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Yes — emphatically. The commanded form of love ('you shall love' — Mat 22:37, Lev 19:18) requires the will's exercise — love cannot be merely a spontaneous feeling if it is commanded. Enemy-love (Mat 5:44, OBS-103-143) is the clearest evidence: loving enemies requires volitional engagement against natural preference. The ranking of loves (Mat 10:37, OBS-103-151) requires a deliberate choice-act — placing love of Christ above natural love of family. Ruth's chesed (Rut 3:10, OBS-103-089) — choosing the elderly Boaz over young men — is love expressed through will against self-interest. The will's engagement is not peripheral but central to love in its highest form (agapē, chesed). The affective forms (phileō, dod) may be less volitionally constituted, but the command-forms require the will. |
| OBS-103-T2-044 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Both operate in the data — love both requires and produces will-acts. It requires the will: enemy-love (Mat 5:44), ranking of loves (Mat 10:37), and Ruth's choice (Rut 3:10) are all volitional love-acts. It produces will-acts: love of Christ produces commandment-keeping (Joh 14:15, OBS-103-140) — the inner orientation generates the volitional pattern of life. The two modes are distinct: love-as-act-of-will (requiring deliberate engagement) vs love-as-generator-of-will (the inner orientation that produces volitional patterns). Both are evidenced. The affective forms (phileō, dod) may operate without explicit volitional initiation, but they too produce will-acts (Peter's fileō generates faithful mission-response, Joh 21:17, OBS-103-153). |
| OBS-103-T2-075 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Love as given outward is the primary ethical direction of the commandment structure: love your neighbour (Lev 19:18), love your enemy (Mat 5:44), love one another (Joh 13:34). The giving of love reaches its maximum in Joh 15:13 (OBS-103-125) — 'greater love has no one than this: that someone lay down his life/soul for his friends.' The gift of the soul is love's outer limit. The giving-forms span: chesed as daily covenantal faithfulness given to the covenant-partner (OBS-103-089); agapē given to the enemy against natural preference (OBS-103-143); phileō given as warmth within the community (OBS-103-119); rachamim given as compassion toward the suffering (OBS-103-105). Love-as-given is the primary ethical form in which the inner orientation is concretely actualised. |
| OBS-103-T2-083 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Yes — explicitly. 1Th 3:12 (OBS-103-130): 'may the Lord make you increase and abound in love' — love admits of increase; the Lord is the agent of its growth. The prayer-form implies that love's increase is both desirable and possible, dependent on the Lord's action. The degree-language in the data also implies growth: Rut 3:10 — 'you have made this last kindness greater than the first' — chesed measurably greater in a later act than an earlier one. Love is not fixed at a static level but admits of deepening expression and increase over time. |
| OBS-103-T2-132 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Already addressed at T5.1 (Q&A-091) and T5.1.2 (Q&A-092). Yes — 1Th 3:12 ('may the Lord make you increase and abound in love'); Rut 3:10 (this chesed greater than the first). Means: divine action (Lord makes it increase), Spirit's work (Gal 5:22), training (Tit 2:4), obedience-pathway (1Pe 1:22). No additional findings beyond Q&A-091 and Q&A-092. |
| OBS-103-T2-154 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Love **enables and extends** agency beyond its natural range. The key evidence: enemy-love (Mat 5:44) produces agency toward those the person would naturally avoid — love extends the agentive reach to include hostile others. Joh 15:13 (OBS-103-125): love produces the maximum agentive act (laying down the soul) that no other motivation could reliably sustain. Ruth's chesed-love (Rut 3:10, OBS-103-089) produces the agentive choice against self-interest — love expands agency by overriding the self-protective constraints that would otherwise limit it. Disordered love produces distorted agency: love-of-money produces the agency of departure from faith (1Ti 6:10); love-of-primacy produces the agency of exclusion (3Jo 9, OBS-103-155). The direction of love determines the direction and quality of agency — love is the inner source from which the person acts outward. |
| OBS-099-T2-006 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Kindness instantiates the outward-oriented relational disposition of God toward others. Mic 6:8 and Eph 4:32 together reveal that the relational-moral complex (justice, kindness, humility; kindness, tenderheartedness, forgiveness) is what the image-bearer embodies [OBS-099-052, OBS-099-077]. The specific divine likeness instantiated: the freedom and generosity to act in favour of another without compulsion — Ruth's kindness to Naomi (Rut 3:10 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]) is the clearest human illustration. |
| OBS-099-T2-007 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Genuinely shared, with structural asymmetry. The same terms (chesed, chrēstos/chrēstotēs) are predicated of both without distinction of kind. 2Sa 22:26 makes the sharing explicit: "With the merciful you show yourself merciful" [XREG: H2616A, OBS-099-096]. Asymmetry: God's chesed is inexhaustible and eschatologically guaranteed; human chesed is creaturely and can be withdrawn (Hos 6:6 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]). The sharing is real; the asymmetry is structural. |
| OBS-099-T2-009 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Yes — three typological movements: (a) Covenantal: each individual divine chesed act participates in and points toward the one covenantal faithfulness underlying them all [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]. (b) Christological: Eph 2:7 names Christ as the supreme display of divine kindness — the eschatological revelation of what chesed has always been pointing toward [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. (c) Human-divine mirror: Ruth's kindness, 1Sa 20:14, 2Sa 10:2 — each human chesed act is typological of the divine pattern [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-070/071]. |
| OBS-099-T2-010 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Primary direction: divine-to-human. Eph 4:32 — "be kind... as God in Christ forgave you" [OBS-099-077]. The divine instance establishes the pattern; human kindness is the derived expression. Secondary direction: human-pointing-to-divine — each human chesed act points toward the divine kindness it participates in. Both directions present; primary movement is from divine instance to human response. |
| OBS-099-T2-016 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Working description: Kindness is a stable inner disposition of loyal goodwill toward another, rooted in the heart, that orients the whole person toward the practical benefit and relational flourishing of its object, regardless of desert. Constituted by faithfulness (holds across time and through failure), compassion (feels with the other), and absence of self-serving motivation. In divine form: inexhaustible, unconditional in ultimate terms, purposively aimed at transformation. In human form: creaturely, finite, Spirit-formed, called to mirror the divine pattern. Primary constitutional location: heart. Primary expression: deliberate relational action. Counterfeit: pleasant speech covering self-serving motivation (chrēstologia). |
| OBS-099-T2-020 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Three modes: (a) Dispositional orientation — chasid is characterised by this as settled personal quality [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. Prior to specific acts; the inner ground from which acts arise. (b) Deliberate act of will — Ruth's graduated kindness, David's covenant-loyalty, Judah's surety-pledge — each a chosen act [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-071, OBS-099-063]. (c) Spirit's interior work — Gal 5:22 — chrēstotēs as fruit of the Spirit; formation mode in which kindness is produced organically [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-092]. Three modes are not mutually exclusive: Spirit forms the disposition; disposition generates deliberate acts. |
| OBS-099-T2-031 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Primary dimension: Relational Disposition (06). Kindness is fundamentally an orientation-toward-another — stable inner stance directed outward toward a relational partner. The chesed vocabulary is constitutively relational: it exists within and sustains covenant. Eight of 27 OWNER groups carry Relational Disposition — largest single dimension. H2617A group 536-001 (180 relevant verses, dominant subject GOD [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083]) confirms at scale. |
| OBS-099-T2-032 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Multi-stranded: (a) Chesed appears overwhelmingly in covenant contexts [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]. (b) All acts of kindness in the data are directed toward specific others [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-063, OBS-099-071, OBS-099-060]. (c) The structural opposite (pride, self-enclosure) is a relational failure [OBS-099-054]. (d) The counterfeit (chrēstologia) fails precisely in the relational direction [OBS-099-074]. Relational Disposition is the correct primary dimension. |
| OBS-099-T2-045 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Yes — two-level movement: (a) Spirit → Soul/Heart: Gal 5:22 (Spirit produces) → Pro 3:3 (written on heart) — Spirit-formation produces heart-level disposition [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-092; XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]. (b) Heart → Relational Action: Pro 3:3 disposition → Rut 3:10 / 2Sa 10:2 / Gen 44:32 — deliberate acts toward others [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-071, OBS-099-063]. Full constitutional movement: Spirit-formation → heart-inscription → relational disposition → deliberate acts. Coherent downward-outward movement from deep to surface, inner to relational. |
| OBS-099-T2-049 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Yes — strongly. (a) Deliberate choice: Rut 3:10 — Ruth's kindness greater than the first — a choice when she did not have to [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]; 2Sa 10:2 — David resolved to show loyalty [OBS-099-071]; Gen 44:32 — Judah placed himself under obligation by choice [OBS-099-063]. (b) Love as volitional act: Mic 6:8 — "love kindness" — love in OT covenant context is a volitional commitment [OBS-099-052]. (c) Putting on: Col 3:12 — "put on... kindness" — deliberate clothing [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Kindness strongly engages the will; it is chosen, not merely felt. |
| OBS-099-T2-053 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Yes — conscientiousness (integrated moral awareness + volition + action) is precisely what kindness requires and produces. The chasid — moral awareness (chesed is right), volition (I will show chesed), action (I do show chesed) integrated into consistent reliable character [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. Pro 3:3 — "let not steadfast love forsake you" — ongoing conscientiousness required [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]. Ruth's graduated kindness shows conscientiousness across multiple acts [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]. Kindness and conscientiousness mutually reinforcing. |
| OBS-099-T2-057 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Well-evidenced. (a) How: specific deliberate acts — Ruth to Naomi (Rut 3:10 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]); David to Hanun (2Sa 10:2 [OBS-099-071]); Jonathan/David (1Sa 20:14 [OBS-099-070]); Judah's surety-pledge (Gen 44:32 [OBS-099-063]). Each concrete, costly, relational. (b) Inner conditions in giver: the chasid — settled inner character [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]; heart-inscription prior to the act (Pro 3:3 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]). (c) What the person must have received: Eph 4:32 — "as God in Christ forgave you, be kind" [OBS-099-077]. Grounded in having received divine kindness/forgiveness first. |
| OBS-117-T2-008 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | The data provides clear evidence in both directions. Presence: the person who has shalom is in right orientation toward God (Isa 26:3 — mind stayed on God = perfect peace; Psa 119:165 — love of God's law = great peace; OBS-117-144). Mal 2:6 (OBS-117-146) — walking in peace and uprightness — is the portrait of the image-bearing priest. Absence: the absence of shalom is diagnostic of inner disorder. Three patterns emerge: (1) Moral disorder → absence of peace: Isa 48:22/57:21 ("there is no peace for the wicked"); the way of peace unknown to those in moral disorder (Isa 59:8); (2) False peace as image corruption: Deu 29:19 (2509-002, OBS-117-150) — the stubborn-hearted person who claims shalom without covenant ground; Jer 6:14/8:11 — prophets crying "peace, peace" when there is no peace; the corrupted image produces false shalom claims; (3) Somatic evidence: Psa 38:3 ("no health in my bones because of my sin") — the absence of shalom-wholeness has bodily expression. The image-bearing conclusion: shalom's presence is the marker of the person in right order with God; its absence (or false claim) reveals image-corruption at the deepest level. |
| OBS-117-T2-016 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | The negative definition is richly evidenced: (1) Peace is not the absence of war alone — the shalom vocabulary covers inner states that have nothing to do with military conflict (Isa 26:3, Psa 4:8, Lam 3:17); (2) Peace is not self-generated security — the false shalom pattern (2509-002, Deu 29:19) shows that self-claimed peace without covenant ground is its precise corruption; (3) Peace is not available to the wicked — Isa 48:22, 57:21 ("there is no peace for the wicked"); the moral character of the person determines whether shalom is accessible; (4) Peace is not silence or passivity — the silence vocabulary (H2790B, H2814, G4623) is distinct from shalom; silence is a condition that enables or expresses peace but is not peace itself; (5) Peace is not complacent ease — sha'anan (H7600 group 413-001) names self-generated ease as a vice (Amo 6:1: "woe to those who are at ease in Zion"), demonstrating that ease without divine ground is not shalom. The negative boundary: peace is not a condition the person achieves by themselves, claims without God's ground, or inhabits while in moral disorder. |
| OBS-117-T2-018 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | Yes — and this is among the most analytically significant features of the peace registry. The false-peace pattern (2508-003 and 2509-002) shows that shalom is precisely the characteristic that can be falsely claimed: (1) False prophecy of shalom: Jer 6:14, 8:11, Eze 13:10, 13:16, Mic 3:5 — prophets cry "Peace, peace" when there is no peace (prior session, incorporated as source material); (2) Self-deceptive shalom: Deu 29:19 (2509-002, OBS-117-150) — "I shall be safe, though I walk in the stubbornness of my heart"; (3) Complacent ease (sha'anan, H7600 group 413-001) — the comfortable feel of worldly security mistaken for shalom; (4) Speaking shalom with evil in the heart: Psa 28:3, Jer 9:8 — "speaks peace to his neighbor while evil is in his heart." The implication: shalom is the most counterfeitable of inner-being characteristics because its absence-of-distress phenomenology can be replicated by complacency, self-deception, or false prophecy. Authentic shalom requires its proper ground (God, covenant, trust) while false shalom is the same phenomenological condition without that ground. |
| OBS-117-T2-021 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | The data provides explicit antonyms: (1) War (milchamah) — Psa 120:7 ("I am for peace, but when I speak, they are for war"); the most direct antonym in the moral-orientation register; (2) Evil (ra'ah) — Jer 29:11 ("plans for welfare and not for evil"); the antonym in the divine-intention register; (3) Calamity/disaster — Isa 45:7 ("I make well-being and create calamity"); the antonym in the creational register; (4) Terror/panic — Jer 30:5 ("a cry of panic, of terror, and no peace"); the experiential antonym; (5) Stubbornness/iniquity — Deu 29:19, Mal 2:6 ("turned many from iniquity"); moral disorder as peace's contrary; (6) Anguish — Eze 7:25 ("when anguish comes they will seek peace, but there shall be none"). The antonym profile confirms that peace is not a neutral absence of distress but a positive condition with multiple contraries — war (relational), evil (moral), calamity (existential), terror (experiential), stubbornness (dispositional). |
| OBS-117-T2-040 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | Yes — with rich evidence. Key heart locations: (1) Deu 29:19 (2509-002 anchor, OBS-117-150) — "blesses himself in his heart [lev]" claiming shalom while walking in "stubbornness of my heart" — false peace is a heart-generated claim; the heart is the seat of the false shalom; (2) 1Ch 12:17 (2510-001 anchor, OBS-117-151) — "my heart will be joined to you" — the shalom of friendship is a heart-bond; (3) Joh 14:27 — "Let not your hearts [kardia] be troubled" — Christ's peace addresses the heart directly; the troubled heart and the peace are antithetical (G1515 426-001 anchor, prior session OBS-117-120); (4) Phili 4:7 — "the peace of God... will guard your hearts [kardia] and your minds" — the heart is the primary guarded territory; (5) Pro 12:20 — "those who plan peace have joy" — planning peace is a heart-act (prior session OBS-117-096 noted the heart-disposition of planning); (6) Psa 28:3 — "speak peace with their neighbors while evil is in their hearts" — heart as the locus of authentic or false orientation toward peace. The data shows 33 co-occurring verses with R183 (heart) — the highest co-occurrence count in the entire registry (G.2, OBS-117-016). |
| OBS-117-T2-041 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | The heart-location of peace engages all four of the heart's integrating functions: (1) Knowing (cognitive): Rom 8:6 ("to set the mind/heart on the Spirit is life and peace") — the heart's cognitive orientation toward God produces peace; the directed heart knows what produces peace; (2) Willing (volitional): Psa 120:7 ("I am for peace") — the heart's volitional orientation toward peace as identity; Isa 30:15 ("but you were unwilling") — the refusal of peace is a heart-act of will; (3) Feeling (affective): Joh 14:27 ("let not your hearts be troubled") — the troubled heart as the experiential opposite of peace; the heart as the seat of distress or rest; (4) Moral awareness (conscience): Deu 29:19 — the heart's stubbornness as the moral condition that generates false shalom; Psa 28:3 — evil in the heart contrasted with speaking peace. The heart is the integrating centre of peace's constitutional address: peace must be received, held, directed, and expressed from the heart. The heart-peace connection is the most comprehensive single-location engagement in the data. |
| OBS-117-T2-071 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | Peace both requires and enables volition: (1) Requires volition: the pursuit of peace (Psa 34:14), the choice to be at peace with others (Rom 12:18 — G1514), the volitional orientation of the mind toward God (Isa 26:3) — all are deliberate acts of will; (2) Enables volition: the quieted person is in a better position to choose rightly — H2790B group 408-001 (holding silence under emotional provocation) shows peace-as-restraint enabling volition over impulse; H2814 group 409-004 (prophetic inner resolve, refusing silence for God's purposes) shows the person of peace being more decisively volitional, not less; (3) False peace bypasses true volition: Deu 29:19 — the self-deceived person's volitional claim ("I shall be safe") is not a genuine act of will directed toward peace but a bypass of the real volitional cost of covenant faithfulness. Peace-bearing volition is the will rightly informed and directed; false peace is the will disengaged from reality. |
| OBS-117-T2-076 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | Yes — extensively. Peace has a moral evaluation function: the presence or absence of shalom is a moral indicator. Evidence: (1) Isa 48:22, 57:21 — "there is no peace for the wicked" — moral condition is the determinative factor for peace's availability; (2) Isa 59:8 — "the way of peace they do not know" — moral disorder produces cognitive/perceptual blindness to peace's way; (3) Mal 2:6 — "walked with me in peace and uprightness" — shalom paired with uprightness as the moral-character portrait; (4) The false-peace pattern (Jer 6:14, Deu 29:19) — the moral faculty has been corrupted when false peace is claimed. Peace's connection to moral evaluation is among the richest in the programme: the peaceful person evaluates rightly; the wicked person cannot perceive peace's way; the self-deceived person evaluates falsely. |
| OBS-117-T2-077 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | Peace enables moral evaluation; its absence and its false form impair it. (1) Enabling: the quiet person evaluates rightly (Pro 14:33 — "wisdom rests in the heart of a man of understanding... but it makes itself known even in the midst of fools" — associated with the quiet-wisdom vocabulary); the peaceful mind (Isa 26:3) is in a better position to discern rightly; (2) False peace impairs: Deu 29:19 — the stubborn heart claiming false shalom "bypasses" the moral evaluation faculty — the person evaluates their own condition falsely because they are in the false-peace condition; Jer 6:14 — the prophets who cry "Peace, peace" have corrupted their moral evaluative function; (3) Absence impairs: Isa 57:20 ("the wicked are like the tossing sea... tossing up mire and dirt") — the restless inner state produces moral opacity, not discernment. |
| OBS-117-T2-078 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | The moral evaluation pattern reveals that peace is both the condition that enables rightly-ordered moral judgment and itself a moral diagnostic tool. The person whose inner life is genuinely at peace is in a position to evaluate moral reality clearly; the person who has claimed false peace is systematically unable to see their condition rightly (Deu 29:19 — "I shall be safe" is the self-description of the self-deceived). This makes peace not only a moral characteristic but an epistemological one in the moral domain — the state of one's peace is a condition of moral perception. |
| OBS-117-T2-079 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | Yes — the connection between peace and conscience is among the most analytically significant in the registry. The primary evidence: (1) Psa 32:3-5 — "when I kept silent, my bones wasted away through my groaning all day long... your hand was heavy upon me" (prior session OBS-117-076 context): suppressed confession = withheld inner acknowledgment of sin = somatic deterioration. The sequence: conscience pressing → person suppressing → bones wasting → person confessing → peace restored (Psa 32:5 — "I acknowledged my sin... and you forgave"). The conscience is the inner faculty whose suppression destroys peace and whose exercise restores it; (2) H2814 group 409-001 — "inward suppression of speech under emotional or spiritual pressure, with deteriorating inner consequence" — conscience-suppression as a pattern; (3) 2509-002 (Deu 29:19) — the false shalom claimed against the conscience's witness; the conscience is what the stubborn heart overrides to claim false peace. |
| OBS-117-T2-080 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | Three conscience-patterns: (1) Suppressed conscience impairs peace (the primary direction): Psa 32:3 — suppressed conscience → bones wasting, no peace; the relationship is causal — withheld confession prevents peace; (2) Exercised conscience enables peace: Psa 32:5 — acknowledged sin → forgiveness → relief; the confession that exercises conscience leads to peace as its outcome; (3) False peace bypasses conscience: Deu 29:19 — the stubborn heart generates false peace by bypassing the conscience's witness; this is the bypass pattern — false shalom is what remains when conscience is overridden. The conscience is thus both the gateway to peace (through confession) and the faculty whose suppression most directly destroys peace (Psa 32:3). No other inner faculty has this bidirectional gateway function as clearly documented in the data. |
| OBS-117-T2-081 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | The conscience pattern reveals that peace and conscience are structurally interdependent: genuine peace requires a functioning and exercised conscience; the suppressed conscience is the primary internal mechanism of peacelessness; and the false peace that bypasses conscience is the most dangerous corruption of the characteristic. Psa 32 is the governing text: the person cannot have peace while suppressing conscience, and when conscience is exercised (confession), peace becomes available. This is a new finding: conscience is the gateway faculty for peace — more than any other inner faculty, the state of the conscience determines whether genuine peace is constitutionally accessible. |
| OBS-117-T2-098 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Deu 29:19 | Enabling conditions: (1) Receptivity — the quieted person is positioned to receive what the peaceful person extends (Psa 131:2 — the weaned-child posture of receptive calm); (2) Genuine seeking: 1Ch 12:17 — "if you have come to me in friendship to help me" — the intention that enables the sha.lom relationship is genuine orientation toward the other's good; (3) Non-hostility: Gen 37:4 — the brothers "could not speak peaceably to Joseph" because of hatred — hatred blocks peace-reception. Blocking conditions: (1) Hatred: Gen 37:4; (2) Stubbornness: Isa 30:15 — the stubborn heart refuses peace even when God offers it; human stubbornness would equally block human-to-human peace-reception; (3) False peace already in place: Deu 29:19 — the person who has self-generated false peace is not available to receive genuine peace from another because they believe they already have it. |
| OBS-060-T2-003 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Job 1:21 | The human giving group (872-005) shows that inner-being giving equips the human person for: (a) receptive relinquishment — accepting that all possessions are gifts held on loan from God (Job 1:21, OBS-060-044); and (b) active covenantal self-offering — giving the body in obedient service grounded in trust (Isa 50:6, OBS-060-045). These together suggest that giving-as-inner-act equips the human for faithful creaturely existence: holding received goods appropriately (as gift, not possession), and offering the self in response to divine call. The gap: the data does not contain explicit creation-order giving — the 872-005 verses are drawn from fallen/covenantal contexts, not from an explicit pre-fall design statement. The purpose of human giving in the created order is not stated; it must be inferred from the functional descriptions in the classified verses. |
| OBS-060-T2-005 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Job 1:21 | The divine image aspect instantiated is the capacity for covenantal self-giving — the ability to give from an inner orientation rather than from compulsion or transaction. God gives from covenantal faithfulness, compassion, and eschatological purpose; the human image-bearer gives from inner relinquishment (Job 1:21, OBS-060-044) and covenantal obedience (Isa 50:6, OBS-060-045). The image-bearing is not in the act of physical giving but in the inner-being orientation that makes the giving an expression of who one is rather than merely what one does. The structural parallel noted in DIM-60-SD001 (OBS-060-033) — divine giving / human giving as echo — is the image-bearing structure: the human reflects the divine pattern of giving-from-inner-commitment. |
| OBS-060-T2-006 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Job 1:21 | The evidence supports shared characteristic with asymmetric form. God gives as the originating and sovereign giver: his giving constitutes covenantal reality (Gen 15:18), endows inner capacities (Neh 9:20, Job 38:36), and enacts eschatological transformation (Isa 61:3). The human gives in responsive mode: Job releases what God gave (Job 1:21); the Servant offers himself in trust of divine vindication (Isa 50:6). The characteristic is genuinely shared — both God and the human give from inner-being orientation — but the asymmetry is structural: God gives primarily and the human gives responsively. Human giving is always downstream of divine giving, either responding to it (Job 1:21) or participating in its pattern (Isa 50:6). This is not exclusively a creaturely analogue — it is a shared characteristic with a directional structure: God → human → God. |
| OBS-060-T2-007 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Job 1:21 | The data provides evidence in both directions. Presence: group 872-005 (OBS-060-022) — human self-giving, sacrificial giving, votive giving as the expression of inner orientation toward God or community. Job 1:21 and Isa 50:6 show the characteristic present in its fullest form: inner relinquishment and bodily self-offering grounded in trust. These are descriptions of the image functioning rightly. Absence/distortion: group 872-004 (OBS-060-021, OBS-060-043) — when faithlessness is met with divine judicial giving-over, the human is described as the object of the giving-over, not the agent of inner giving. The judicial texts imply a prior failure of inner-being orientation. Group 872-006 provides the clearest inverse: failure to give glory to God (Jer 13:16, OBS-060-047) produces inner-being darkening — darkness, stumbling, gloom. The absence of glory-giving reveals an inner-being disorder, not merely a behavioural lapse. |
| OBS-060-T2-013 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | The data reveals a dual structure: faithfulness in this registry is simultaneously a disposition and an act. The inner-being disposition is the prior commitment or orientation (covenant faithfulness in 872-001, compassion in 872-002, inner trust in 872-005) from which the act of giving proceeds. The giving itself is the act — the enacted, external form of the inner disposition. Neither alone constitutes faithfulness as the data describes it: the disposition without the act remains unfulfilled commitment; the act without the disposition is mere transaction. The group landscape (OBS-060-025) confirms this: each group description names both an inner-being source (covenant faithfulness, love and compassion, inner orientation toward God) and an enacted expression (giving land, giving in response to need, self-giving). This is best described as a disposition-in-action: an inner-being quality that is only fully itself when expressed outwardly. This is consistent with DIM-60-001 (OBS-060-032, OBS-060-053). |
| OBS-060-T2-015 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | Working description: Faithfulness in this registry is the inner-being quality by which a person (primarily God, secondarily the human image-bearer) enacts their inner commitments, orientations, and compassions through giving — giving that is always relational, directed, and sourced in an inner-being state rather than in external obligation. It operates at the intersection of moral character (covenant keeping), relational disposition (compassion enacted), cognition (endowing inner capacities), transformation (eschatological reversal of inner states), and divine-human correspondence (glory-ascription in its proper direction). The primary faculty engagement is the whole person acting from within: the heart (leb) as source (Hos 11:8), the soul (nephesh) as the seat of attachment that motivates giving (Jer 12:7), the spirit (ruach) as both the object of endowing-giving (Neh 9:20) and the faculty transformed by eschatological-giving (Isa 61:3). Impact on the person: receiving divine inner-being giving produces trust, praise, transformed inner states, and restored responsiveness; giving from inner orientation produces alignment with God and participation in the divine pattern of faithful action. |
| OBS-060-T2-016 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | The data provides evidence of two structural opposites, corresponding to the two directions of giving in the registry. (1) For divine giving: withholding not from compassion but from covenantal abandonment — the inverse of faithful giving is the withdrawal of covenantal presence and provision. Hos 11:8 (OBS-060-038) holds the tension: divine compassion prevents the giving-over, but the possibility of giving-over represents the opposite of covenantal giving. Group 872-004 (OBS-060-021) — judicial giving-over as moral response to faithlessness — is not the structural opposite of 872-001 but its dark mirror: the same covenant enacted in judgement. (2) For human giving: the structural opposite is possessiveness or hoarding — treating given goods as one's own possession rather than as received gifts. Job 1:21 (OBS-060-044) makes this implicit: the naked return inverts the possessive claim. The failure to give glory (Jer 13:16, OBS-060-047) — withholding from God what is his — is the clearest stated opposite in the corpus, producing inner-being darkness. |
| OBS-060-T2-019 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | The data reveals five distinct operational modes (OBS-060-025, OBS-060-051). (1) Performative giving: the act of giving constitutes a covenantal reality — "I will give this land" enacts the covenant (OBS-060-016, OBS-060-036). (2) Responsive giving: giving triggered by the recipient's inner condition — God gives in direct answer to suffering/need (OBS-060-037). (3) Endowing giving: giving that deposits inner-being capacity — Spirit, wisdom, understanding given directly to the inner life (OBS-060-040, OBS-060-041). (4) Judicial giving: giving as moral consequence — giving-over as the expression of inner moral response to faithlessness (OBS-060-042, OBS-060-043). (5) Transformative giving: giving that reverses inner-being states — replacing depletion with fullness, mourning with gladness (OBS-060-048, OBS-060-049, OBS-060-050). In the human direction, a sixth mode is also present: (6) Relinquishing giving: releasing what was received, recognising divine sovereignty over all given goods (OBS-060-044). |
| OBS-060-T2-022 | VERSE_PATTERN | Job 1:21 | The most consistently evidenced immediate response to receiving divine inner-being giving is worship — specifically the altar-building response in Gen 12:7 (OBS-060-035: "he built there an altar to the Lord, who had appeared to him") immediately following the covenantal giving. Job 1:21 (OBS-060-044) shows the immediate response to divine giving and taking as blessing: "blessed be the name of the Lord." Hos 2:15 (OBS-060-050) shows inner responsiveness restored — "she shall answer" — as the immediate sign of received eschatological giving. The pattern across these instances: receiving divine inner-being giving produces immediate inner-being reorientation toward God — worship, blessing, responsive answering. The receiving of endowed inner capacity (Neh 9:20, OBS-060-040) does not show an explicit immediate response in the verse itself, but the purpose given (to instruct) implies the response is formation and obedience. |
| OBS-060-T2-023 | VERSE_PATTERN | Job 1:21 | The immediate response varies by the mode of giving received. (1) Covenantal giving → worship/altar-building (Gen 12:7). (2) Compassionate giving (child given to Leah) → naming/attribution of divine hearing (Gen 29:33, OBS-060-037) — the name Simeon memorialises the inner act of divine hearing and giving. (3) Judicial giving-over → no inner-being response recorded in the anchor verses — the judicial act proceeds without a recorded human response in the primary texts. (4) Eschatological giving → restored responsiveness/praise (Hos 2:15, Isa 61:3). (5) Divine giving-and-taking → blessing-under-loss (Job 1:21). The variation is directionally consistent: where receiving is welcome, the response is worship/praise/gratitude; where receiving is judicial, there is silence on response (suggesting the human is the object, not the agent). The variation is meaningful, not random. |
| OBS-060-T2-028 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Job 1:21 | The data provides partial evidence. For covenantal giving (872-001): the condition evidenced is being in a covenant relationship with God — the giving of land is addressed to Abraham and his offspring, not to all humanity indiscriminately. The covenant relationship is the precondition of reception. For compassionate giving (872-002): the condition evidenced is the presenting of genuine inner need — Leah is hated and suffering; God hears and gives (OBS-060-037). Expressed need or suffering appears to enable responsive giving. For eschatological giving (872-007): no precondition is named in the anchor verses — those who mourn receive beauty (Isa 61:3), suggesting mourning itself is the enabling condition. For human self-giving (Job 1:21, Isa 50:6): the enabling inner condition is trust/covenantal identity — Job's worship proceeds from recognising divine sovereignty; the Servant gives himself because he trusts divine vindication. Gap: the data does not address whether ongoing faithfulness or human moral condition affects the receptivity to divine covenantal giving — the texts name the giving as God's initiative, not as contingent on human merit. |
| OBS-060-T2-037 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Job 1:21 | Yes — the nephesh (soul) appears explicitly in Jer 12:7 (OBS-060-042): "the beloved of my soul (yedidut nafshi)" — God's nephesh is invoked in connection with the giving-over. This is divine soul-level language in direct connection with na.tan. For the human direction: Job 1:21 (OBS-060-044) does not use nephesh explicitly but the verse addresses the whole person at the level of deepest identity — the naked-returning self who blesses God's name. The soul-level register is implicit in Job's response: the relinquishment and worship described are total-person acts that engage the deepest level of self. The Servant's self-giving in Isa 50:6 similarly does not use nephesh explicitly but the bodily self-offering is an act of the whole person. Soul-level location is most clearly evidenced in the divine direction via Jer 12:7. |
| OBS-060-T2-038 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Job 1:21 | The soul-level evidence (Jer 12:7 — divine nephesh in connection with giving-over) reveals that inner-being giving at its deepest level is connected to the innermost personal experience of attachment and love. God's giving-over proceeds from — and is described in terms of — the beloved relationship at the soul level. "Beloved of my soul" is the language of deepest personal attachment; the giving-over is not cold judicial execution but an act that costs God at the soul level. This reveals that faithfulness-as-giving is not merely a moral or relational act — it touches the innermost identity of the giver. For the human person, the relinquishing giving of Job 1:21 similarly engages the innermost self: the person who blesses God under devastating loss is acting from the deepest constitutional level, not from surface composure. Soul-level location confirms that this characteristic is not merely behavioural — it is constitutionally deep. |
| OBS-060-T2-053 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Job 1:21 | The origin is multiple — and the multiplicity is structured by subject and mode (Q&A-062). For God: singular internal origin (divine inner-being character). For the human: multiple. The inner orientation that grounds human giving is received (formed by prior divine giving, covenant relationship, Spirit-endowment) — but it is expressed as though from within (Job's response is his own inner act, not a mechanically caused output). The multiplicity of origin reflects the characteristic's dual structure as disposition-in-action: the disposition's origin may be received while the act's origin is genuinely internal to the person. The data does not collapse this into a single origin. |
| OBS-060-T2-054 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Job 1:21 | Yes — the origin shifts across contexts as follows. In covenantal giving contexts (872-001): origin is divine internal character, unconditional and self-generated. In responsive giving (872-002): origin is divine compassion triggered by the human's presented need — the origin is still internal to God but responsive to external condition. In endowing giving (872-003): origin is divine initiative depositing capacity in the human — giving constitutes the human's inner capacity from the outside. In judicial giving-over (872-004): origin is divine moral response to human faithlessness — external condition (human behaviour) shapes the mode of divine giving. In human self-giving (872-005): origin is the human's inner trust and acceptance, formed by but distinct from received divine giving. The characteristic's origin is not static — it is context-sensitive across the seven groups, reflecting the full range of divine-human interaction. |
| OBS-060-T2-066 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | Yes — prominently. The affective faculty is engaged in multiple groups. (1) Divine affect as source of giving: Hos 11:8 (OBS-060-038) — compassion (rahamim) grows warm and tender, arresting the giving-over. The divine affective movement is the determinant of giving. Hos 13:11 (OBS-060-043) — anger and wrath as the inner-being states from which judicial giving proceeds. (2) Affect as the object of eschatological giving: Isa 61:3 (OBS-060-048) — mourning replaced by gladness (oil of gladness); sorrow replaced by praise. Affect (mourning, gladness) is both the presenting inner state and the transformed inner state — the eschatological giving directly addresses and transforms emotional experience. (3) Human affect in self-giving: Job 1:21 — blessing under devastating loss is an affective-volitional act. |
| OBS-060-T2-067 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | The characteristic both enables and transforms affect. In its eschatological mode (872-007): the transformative giving directly replaces negative affect (mourning, faint spirit) with positive affect (gladness, praise) — Isa 61:3 (OBS-060-048). This is direct affective transformation: the giving enables new affective states that were previously absent or depleted. In its responsive mode (872-002): the divine affective state (rahamim, compassion — Hos 11:8, OBS-060-038) is itself what the giving expresses — the characteristic does not bypass affect but is grounded in it. The human relinquishing mode (Job 1:21, OBS-060-044) shows affect operating under the characteristic: blessing and worship under devastating loss is an affective act, not a stoic suppression of affect. The characteristic does not impair affect in any evidenced instance; where it fails to operate (glory withheld from God), affect is disordered (Jer 13:16 — darkness, stumbling). Affect is constitutively engaged throughout. |
| OBS-060-T2-069 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | Yes — volition is central to the characteristic in multiple modes. (1) Divine giving as volitional act: the giving of land and covenant is an act of divine will — God chose to give, to whom, and what. The covenantal giving is a decision, not a mechanical output. Most explicitly in Hos 11:8 (OBS-060-038): "How can I give you up?" — the divine volition is in tension with itself, resolved by compassion. The heart-recoiling is a volitional-affective movement. (2) Human self-giving (872-005): Job 1:21 (OBS-060-044) — the decision to bless God under devastating loss is a volitional act of the highest order. Isa 50:6 (OBS-060-045) — "I gave my back" — active volitional self-offering. (3) The imperative in Jer 13:16 ("give glory to the Lord your God") directly addresses human volition — the giving of glory is commanded, presupposing that it can be chosen or refused. |
| OBS-060-T2-070 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | The characteristic deepens volition in its human expression (Jobs 1:21, Isa 50:6) — the act of self-giving under loss or suffering represents a deepened volitional capacity, not a constrained one. The Servant's giving of himself (Isa 50:6) is freely chosen. For divine volition: Hos 11:8 shows divine volition under constraint from within — the compassion constrains the judicial will, producing the restraint of giving-over. This is not impairment of divine volition but its full expression through the integration of affective-volitional movement. In the receiving direction: the imperative to give glory (Jer 13:16) presupposes full volitional capacity in the human — the command would be meaningless if volition were bypassed. The characteristic neither bypasses nor impairs human volition; it calls it forth and elevates it. |
| OBS-060-T2-071 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | The pattern reveals that inner-being giving requires and expresses genuine volition — it cannot be coerced or mechanically produced. The imperative form (give glory), the contemplated act (how can I give you up?), and the active self-offerings (I gave my back; blessed be the name of the Lord) are all volitional structures. This distinguishes the characteristic sharply from passive reception — even the human who receives divine giving must volitionally receive it (Q&A-031 showed that reception requires inner conditions). The pattern further reveals that faithfulness-as-giving is an exercise of the full volitional capacity in alignment with inner-being commitment — volition in service of prior covenantal orientation. |
| OBS-060-T2-072 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | Yes — agency is the primary external form of this characteristic. Every instance of na.tan in the inner-being register is an act — something is given, transferred, offered. Agency is intrinsic: the giving requires an agent who initiates and performs the transfer. In the divine direction: God acts to give land (Gen 12:7), Spirit (Neh 9:20), transformative gifts (Isa 61:3) — all are divine initiatives, sovereign acts of divine agency. In the human direction: Job acts (blesses — Job 1:21), the Servant acts (gives his back — Isa 50:6), humans are commanded to act (give glory — Jer 13:16). The characteristic is not merely a state or disposition — it is always an acted-out disposition. Agency is the faculty through which the inner-being orientation becomes external and real. |
| OBS-060-T2-074 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | Agency is not incidental to the characteristic — it is constitutive. The disposition-in-action structure (Q&A-016) requires agency: the inner-being orientation only becomes faithfulness when it produces action. The pattern reveals that this characteristic exists at the intersection of inner orientation and outward act — agency is the bridge. The endowing giving further reveals that where agency is depleted or absent (faint spirit, darkened perception), divine giving restores it. This implies that the characteristic has a self-sustaining property in the divine direction: God's giving enables the human capacity to respond and give in turn, which sustains the relational dynamic. |
| OBS-060-T2-081 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | Yes — the human self-giving group (872-005) is the clearest instance of conscientiousness engaged. Job 1:21 (OBS-060-044): moral awareness (the Lord gives and takes), volition (blessed be the name of the Lord), and action (worship) are integrated in a single response. This is conscientiousness in its fullest form — the person knows the right, chooses it, and enacts it under the most extreme testing conditions. Isa 50:6 (OBS-060-045): moral awareness (covenantal obedience), volition (I gave), and action (bodily self-offering) are similarly integrated. The glory-giving imperative (Jer 13:16) also addresses conscientiousness: it calls for integrated moral awareness, volitional response, and enacted glorification. |
| OBS-060-T2-082 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | The characteristic deepens conscientiousness in its endowing mode (Spirit and wisdom given constitute the moral-cognitive faculties that conscientiousness draws on) and demands it in its volitional-expressive mode (Job's blessing, the Servant's self-offering, the glory-imperative all require and express full conscientiousness). Covenantal narrative (Neh 9) — the memory of divine giving — deepens conscientiousness by grounding it in a received moral-relational framework. The judicial giving-over (872-004) is in one sense the consequence of failed conscientiousness: the failure to integrate moral awareness, will, and action in covenant faithfulness results in divine moral response. The characteristic therefore both demands and enables conscientiousness — it does not bypass it. |
| OBS-060-T2-083 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | The pattern reveals that faithfulness-as-giving is, at its human apex, an expression of complete conscientiousness — the full integration of moral awareness, will, and action. Job 1:21 and Isa 50:6 are the programme's clearest examples of conscientiousness under pressure: the person who knows rightly, wills rightly, and acts rightly in the face of overwhelming cost. The characteristic reveals that genuine inner-being giving is impossible without conscientiousness — it requires the integration of all three elements. This confirms the registered dimension of Moral Character: the characteristic is not merely an act of goodwill but a full moral-character expression. It also explains why the failure mode (faithlessness leading to giving-over) is so structurally significant — failing conscientiousness breaks the inner-being circuit that makes genuine giving possible. |
| OBS-060-T2-084 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | Yes — relational capacity is intrinsic to the characteristic. Every classified use of na.tan in the inner-being register involves a giver and a receiver in genuine relational engagement. Group 872-002 most directly engages relational capacity: God gives in direct response to Leah's inner condition of being hated (Gen 29:33, OBS-060-037) — this presupposes divine attentiveness to the relational situation of the other. Hos 11:8 (OBS-060-038) shows the relational depth of divine giving: the heart recoils, compassion grows warm — these are movements of genuine relational connection, not administrative decision. For the human: Isa 50:6 — the Servant's self-giving is an act of relational fidelity. Job 1:21 — the relinquishing blessing is addressed to the Lord (not internal) — it is a relational act toward God. Group 872-006 (glory-giving) is structurally about the proper orientation of the divine-human relational connection. |
| OBS-060-T2-086 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | The pattern reveals that this characteristic is constitutively relational — it cannot be abstracted from the two-party structure of giver and receiver. Every mode of inner-being giving in the registry is a relational act: covenantal commitment is always toward a covenantal partner; compassionate giving is always responsive to someone's condition; judicial giving-over is always addressed at a covenant relationship that has broken; glory-giving is always directional toward God. The characteristic builds, sustains, and — in its eschatological mode — restores relational capacity. This confirms the group 872-002 and 872-005 Relational Disposition dimension assignments and deepens the understanding of why the registry's dimensional breadth spans Moral Character and Relational Disposition: the characteristic is moral in its source (covenantal commitment) and relational in its expression (always enacted toward another). |
| OBS-060-T2-090 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Job 1:21 | Yes — group 872-005 and 872-006 together evidence the human-to-God direction. (1) Glory-giving (872-006): Jer 13:16 (OBS-060-047) — humans commanded to give glory to God. This is a directed movement of the human inner person toward God: the act of ascribing worth, acknowledging God's reality, orienting the inner life Godward. Isa 42:8 — God declares his glory is not given to another, establishing the proper human direction. (2) Worship (872-005): Gen 12:7 — Abram builds an altar immediately after receiving the covenantal promise. This worship-response is the human giving of self in gratitude and recognition. Job 1:21 (OBS-060-044) — "blessed be the name of the Lord" — blessing God under loss is a human-to-God giving of praise and acknowledgement. (3) Sacrificial self-offering (872-005): Isa 50:6 (OBS-060-045) — the Servant's self-giving is performed in the context of covenantal obedience and trust in divine vindication — a movement of the human self toward God through enacted fidelity. |
| OBS-060-T2-091 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Job 1:21 | Three inner postures are evidenced for the human-to-God direction. (1) Recognition of divine sovereignty: Job 1:21 — "the Lord gave, the Lord has taken away" — the inner posture is one of acknowledged divine ownership of all that was received. The human who gives glory or blessing to God is one who recognises that what they have was always his. (2) Trust in divine vindication: Isa 50:6 — the Servant's self-giving is sustained by trust (the subsequent Isa 50:7-9 context) that God will vindicate. The inner posture is covenantal confidence: giving the self to suffering because the covenant relationship with God is reliable. (3) Urgency-recognition: Jer 13:16 — "before darkness comes" — the inner posture for glory-giving is one that recognises the temporal urgency of proper relational orientation. The human must give glory before inner-being disorder sets in. |
| OBS-060-T2-092 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Job 1:21 | The human-to-God giving direction reveals that the relationship with God is one of responsive correspondence: the human gives back to God (glory, praise, self) in direct correspondence to what God has first given. Job's blessing mirrors divine giving-and-taking. The Servant's self-offering mirrors divine covenantal faithfulness. The glory-giving (Jer 13:16) returns to God what is already his. The relationship is not symmetrical — God gives primarily, the human gives responsively — but it is genuinely reciprocal in form. The inner postures required (recognition, trust, urgency) reveal that the relationship requires the full engagement of the human inner person — it is not a passive receiving relationship but an active responsive one. The DHC (Divine-Human Correspondence) dimension of group 872-006 (OBS-060-023) is the clearest expression: the correspondence is the relational structure constituted by the proper flow of giving in both directions. |
| OBS-060-T2-094 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | From the evidence: (1) Recognition of prior reception: the human who gives genuinely does so from an inner awareness of what was first received — Job's giving of blessing proceeds from his recognition that the Lord gave first (Job 1:21). The giver is one who knows themselves as recipient. (2) Inner orientation toward the other: group 872-005 describes "inner orientation toward God or community" as the source of genuine giving — the giving is not incidental or obligatory but flows from a directed inner disposition. (3) Trust in the relational framework: the Servant's self-giving (Isa 50:6) is enabled by trust in divine vindication — the giver can give the self because the relational covenant with God is reliable. (4) Volitional-moral integration: conscientiousness (Q&A-095 through Q&A-097) — the alignment of moral awareness, will, and act — is what enables genuine giving rather than performance. |
| OBS-060-T2-095 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Job 1:21 | The data implies a prior-reception structure for genuine human giving. Job 1:21 establishes it most clearly: "the Lord gave" precedes and grounds the blessing. The human giver must have first received — must know themselves as recipient of divine giving — before their own giving is grounded in this characteristic rather than in self-generated generosity. The Servant's self-giving (Isa 50:6) is grounded in received covenantal identity and received promise of vindication (Isa 50:7-9). The person must have received: (a) covenantal identity (participation in the narrative of divine faithfulness), (b) inner-being formation through Spirit/wisdom endowment, and (c) the capacity to recognise divine sovereignty over all possessions. Without these prior receptions, giving remains transactional. This is consistent with the image-bearing structure (Q&A-008): the human gives responsively, and responsive giving requires prior reception. |
| OBS-060-T2-104 | VERSE_PATTERN | Job 1:21 | Yes — the characteristic produces transformation in both modes across different groups. (1) Condition change: eschatological giving (872-007) changes inner-being conditions directly — ashes replaced by beauty, mourning replaced by gladness, faint spirit replaced by garment of praise (Isa 61:3, OBS-060-048). This is condition change: the person's inner state is materially different after receiving the giving. Hos 2:15 — the Valley of Achor transformed into a door of hope — changes both the external condition (the place) and the inner-being condition (shame → hope). (2) Orientation change: Job 1:21 — Job's condition (devastation) does not change through the giving, but his orientation to it changes — blessing under loss is an orientation-to-condition change. The covenantal giving (872-001) changes the condition of landlessness to land-possession while also forming the orientation of covenant identity. The evidence shows both modes: condition change (primary in 872-007) and orientation change (primary in 872-005, also present in 872-001). |
| OBS-060-T2-110 | VERSE_PATTERN | Job 1:21 | Yes — suffering is a pervasive context for the characteristic across three groups. (1) As the trigger for compassionate giving (872-002): Leah's suffering (being hated) triggers divine giving (Gen 29:33, OBS-060-037). Hosea's Israel suffering from lack, drought, and need is the context of giving in the broader 872-002 corpus. (2) As the context within which human self-giving is enacted (872-005): the Servant gives his back to those who strike — bodily suffering is the medium of the self-giving (Isa 50:6, OBS-060-045). Job's devastating losses are the context of his blessing-response (Job 1:21, OBS-060-044). (3) As the condition that eschatological giving addresses (872-007): "those who mourn in Zion" — mourning/grief is the before-state that the eschatological giving transforms (Isa 61:3, OBS-060-048). The characteristic operates as: response to suffering (compassionate mode), act within suffering (self-giving mode), and remedy for suffering (eschatological mode). |
| OBS-060-T2-111 | VERSE_PATTERN | Job 1:21 | Suffering reveals and tests the characteristic most clearly. Job 1:21 is paradigmatic: the loss of everything reveals whether inner relinquishment and worship-under-loss is genuine. The suffering does not produce the characteristic — it exposes whether it is present. The Servant's self-giving (Isa 50:6) similarly: the suffering reveals the depth of covenantal trust and inner commitment. The suffering does not generate the trust — but it is the context in which trust's reality is proven. In the eschatological mode: suffering (mourning, faint spirit) is the before-state that the divine giving addresses — in this case suffering is neither the producer nor the test but the condition that occasions the transformative giving. The characteristic is therefore suffering-tested (revealed by suffering) in the human direction and suffering-responsive (triggered by suffering) in the divine direction. |
| OBS-060-T2-117 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Job 1:21 | Using the G.2 co-occurrence data (OBS-060-029, OBS-060-030): the strongest adjacent characteristics by shared verse count are R099 kindness (12 shared verses), R183 heart (12), R180 yielding (11), R204 name (11), R197 authority (9), R187 strength (8), R019 calling (7), R034 covenant (7). The anchor verse analysis confirms four of these as analytically significant: R034 covenant (directly present in groups 872-001 and 872-004 through the covenant structure), R183 heart (leb in Hos 11:8 and Neh 9:8), R099 kindness/hesed (structural overlap with compassionate giving), R121 praise (shared anchor Isa 61:3 — five shared anchor registries). Additionally R023 compassion (shared anchor Hos 11:8), R103 love (shared anchor Jer 12:7), R194 blessing and R206 vulnerability (shared anchor Job 1:21). |
| OBS-060-T2-122 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Job 1:21 | The human expression of inner-being giving (872-005) is produced by two prior characteristics. (1) Trust/faith (R059): the Servant's self-giving (Isa 50:6) is grounded in trust in divine vindication — the giving is enabled by the prior state of trust. Without trust, the self-giving does not occur. (2) Received divine giving: Job's blessing-under-loss (Job 1:21) is grounded in the prior recognition of divine giving — "the Lord gave" is the necessary prior statement. The human giving is produced by awareness of received giving. At the structural level: divine giving (covenantal faithfulness) is produced by — or more precisely, expresses — divine love (R103), compassion (R023), and moral character. These inner-being qualities of God are the source from which the giving proceeds. Giving is the enacted form of these prior characteristics. |
| OBS-060-T2-130 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Job 1:21 | Yes — 12 shared anchor verses confirmed in G.3 (OBS-060-031, OBS-060-034 through OBS-060-038). Key shared anchors: Isa 61:3 (R006 anointing, R071 grief, R121 praise, R151 sorrow), Hos 11:8 (R023 compassion, R120 perverseness, R151 sorrow), Job 1:21 (R194 blessing, R206 vulnerability), Jer 12:7 (R103 love), Gen 12:7 (R058 experience), Job 38:36 (R183 heart). These shared anchors are the most cross-registry dense verses in the registry. Isa 61:3 anchors five other registries alongside R060 — the highest density. |
| OBS-060-T2-131 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Job 1:21 | Each shared anchor reveals a specific cross-registry relationship. (1) Isa 61:3 shared with R006, R071, R121, R151: the transformative-giving text simultaneously engages anointing, grief, praise, and sorrow — this verse is a structural node where multiple characteristics converge. Giving is the act that binds them: the anointing, the release from grief, the gift of praise, the end of sorrow are all forms of divine giving in the same verse. (2) Hos 11:8 shared with R023, R120, R151: compassion, perverseness, and sorrow are all present in the divine restraint-from-giving-over verse — the giving is withheld because of compassion, and is provoked by perverseness, in the context of grief. These three characteristics define the relational situation that determines the giving. (3) Job 1:21 shared with R194, R206: blessing and vulnerability are both products of the same experience of divine giving-and-taking — the verse holds them together. (4) Jer 12:7 shared with R103: love and judicial giving are structurally fused — the giving-over proceeds from love. Each shared anchor reveals a genuine inter-characteristic relationship, not coincidence. |
| OBS-060-T2-151 | OBSERVATION | Job 1:21 | Attachment theory surfaces one under-addressed aspect: the experience of the person receiving divine giving when it is not expected or understood — the phenomenology of received divine faithfulness from the recipient's perspective. The verse evidence shows divine giving and its immediate worship-response (Gen 12:7), but the rich subjective experience of being reliably given-to over time — the development of secure inner-being orientation through the pattern of received faithfulness — is not directly narrated in the anchor verses. This is the attachment security dimension: how repeated reliable giving forms the inner-being expectation of trustworthiness that enables risk-taking and self-giving in return. The verse evidence implies this (Job's response, the Servant's trust) but does not narrate it phenomenologically. Further verse investigation would be needed in the wisdom texts (particularly the Psalms) which narrate the experiential dimension of received divine faithfulness. This gap does not require Stage 2a reopening — it requires acknowledgement as an area not covered by the classified corpus of this registry. |

### H3190 ya.tav — be good (`mti_id=632`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H3190` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R186 gladness · Legacy C-cluster: C03
3. Sub-group: —
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 117
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=632` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`

**Verses:** 110 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (3)**
