|---|---|---|---|
| 2837 | 632-001 | Term names the heart being glad or merry — the inner state of lightness and well-being expressed in festive eating, drinking, and communal celebration | Revised during Dimension Review Phase B: description was too vague. Revised from anchor verse context and registry-level observations. |
| 2838 | 632-002 | Ya.tav as moral goodness and doing good: inner moral orientation |  |
| 2839 | 632-003 | Term names the inner state of being pleased or finding something good — the approval, satisfaction, or favour that the inner person extends toward a person, course of action, or situation | Revised during Dimension Review Phase B: description was too vague. Revised from anchor verse context and registry-level observations. |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (1)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-186-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Jdg 19:6; Psa 34:8; Gen 1:31; Deu 6:18 | Ya.tav (H3190) produces three materially distinct dimension patterns: Affective/Emotional (merry heart), Moral/Conscience (moral goodness), and Affective/Emotional (approval/pleasure). This bridges the moral and affective dimensions within a single gladness/goodness term. Session B should examine whether the Hebrew gladness/goodness vocabulary reveals an integration of moral and affective categories that the programme's dimension vocabulary treats separately. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (56 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| 182-F007 | VERSE_PATTERN | Jer 32:41 | God employs soul-vocabulary for his own inner states: Exo 31:17 (God refreshed — na.phash), Isa 42:1 (God's soul delights), Jer 32:41 (God's whole-soul commitment). Soul-language is the available language for divine inner states analogous to human inner states — not asserting God has a creaturely soul but using soul vocabulary analogically for divine pathos. |
| DIM-186-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Deu 6:18 | Ya.tav (H3190) produces three materially distinct dimension patterns: Affective/Emotional (merry heart), Moral/Conscience (moral goodness), and Affective/Emotional (approval/pleasure). This bridges the moral and affective dimensions within a single gladness/goodness term. Session B should examine whether the Hebrew gladness/goodness vocabulary reveals an integration of moral and affective categories that the programme's dimension vocabulary treats separately. |
| OBS-067-T2-001 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 119:68 | The verse evidence reveals that goodness is constitutive of God's character at its most fundamental level. Psa 119:68 ("You are good and do good") establishes an ontological prior — God's being-good precedes and grounds his doing-good. This is not goodness as one attribute among many but as a character description. The doxological assertion group (884-001) — "the Lord is good, his steadfast love endures forever" — names goodness as the summative confession of who God is in his covenantal relationship to creation. Gen 1:31 (884-007) extends this: the Creator's evaluative pronouncement declaring creation "very good" is an expression of his own goodness — he can only declare good what coheres with his character. God's goodness thus images itself in the human person as the capacity for moral assessment, relational orientation, and ontological alignment with what genuinely benefits. The human image-bearer who is good reflects the same character that God is, not by self-generation but by derivation. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-036, OBS-067-OBS-037) |
| OBS-067-T2-002 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 119:68 | Scripture attributes goodness to God extensively and explicitly. Psa 119:68 ("You are good"), Psa 34:8 ("the Lord is good"), Psa 100:5 and Ps 106:1 / 107:1 / 118:1 (doxological refrain "for he is good"), Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him"), Rom 11:22 (God's chrēstotēs), Eph 2:7 (God's chrēstotēs in Christ). The attribution reveals that goodness in the human person is not an independent achievement but a participation in and reflection of an attribute that originates in God. Human goodness is derivative by design — it is what being made in the image of the good God looks like in a creature. This has direct significance: the human person who embodies goodness is expressing their created nature; the human person who fails to do so is in a condition of departure from original design. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-006) |
| OBS-067-T2-006 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 119:68 | Goodness expresses the divine image in three aspects. First, the moral-evaluative aspect: God judges what is good (Gen 1:31 — "God saw that it was good"); the human person made in God's image likewise has the capacity to assess, judge, and recognise what is genuinely good (884-004 — comparative wisdom; 884-006 — moral verdict). Second, the character aspect: God is good in his being (Psa 119:68 — "you are good"); the human image-bearer who is good in character (884-002) expresses this aspect of God's likeness. Third, the relational-missional aspect: God does good (Psa 119:68 — "and do good"); the human image-bearer extends goodness toward others (Mic 6:8; Rom 15:14). The structural parallel is: God is good → does good → declares good; the human image-bearer is made good → called to do good → to assess what is good. The image instantiation is comprehensive — encompassing character, conduct, and cognition. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-036) |
| OBS-067-T2-007 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 119:68 | The evidence supports genuine sharing of the same lexical and moral category — tov names both God's goodness and human goodness without semantic differentiation in H2896A. Psa 119:68 addresses God as good and then asks God to teach statutes so the human can also know and do good — the same category of goodness applies to both. G5544 (chrēstotēs) is explicitly shared: it names God's inner disposition (886-001, Rom 11:22; Eph 2:7) and the Spirit-produced quality of the believer (886-002, Gal 5:22; Col 3:12) using the identical term. This is not merely analogy — the NT explicitly frames human chrēstotēs as Spirit-produced, meaning the divine goodness is the active source of its human expression (not merely the pattern for it). The relationship is participatory: human goodness participates in divine goodness rather than merely imitating it. However, Gen 1:31 (the creative-pronouncement group, 884-007) has no human analogue — only God can evaluate and declare creation good in that ontological-constitutive sense. (OBS-067-OBS-017, OBS-067-OBS-037, OBS-067-OBS-045) |
| OBS-067-T2-010 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 119:68 | The direction is consistently divine-to-human. Mic 6:8 makes this explicit: "He has told you, O man, what is good" — the definition of goodness flows from God to the human person. Gen 1:31 establishes goodness as a divine creative-evaluative reality before any human instance exists. Psa 119:68 ("You are good... teach me") confirms the direction: human goodness is learned from and patterned on divine goodness. The divine instance is originating and pattern-setting; the human instance is derived, responsive, and participatory. There is no movement in the data where human goodness is the type pointing to a divine antitype. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-026) |
| OBS-067-T2-011 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Psa 119:68 | The programme calls this characteristic "goodness." The name signals at the outset that this is a quality-word — a substantive descriptor of what something or someone genuinely is — rather than an action-word or a relation-word. "Goodness" implies a settled, characterological condition rather than an occasional act. The name carries an evaluative implication: to be good is to be assessed and found genuinely beneficial, right, and aligned with the standard of the good. The programme's naming decision appropriately captures the H2896A dominant register (adjectival quality — tov) and the Greek agathōsunē (abstract noun of the agathos root — goodness as a named inner virtue). What the name does not capture on its face is the semantic breadth that the actual vocabulary reveals: tov covers aesthetic pleasantness, moral rightness, covenantal reliability, volitional preference, and inner well-being in a way the English "goodness" does not. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-088) |
| OBS-067-T2-013 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Psa 119:68 | "Goodness" carries all three implications. Directional: the name points upward (to God as the origin and standard of goodness) and outward (to others as the recipients of goodness extended). Goodness is not self-enclosed — its very definition as beneficial implies a direction of benefit-toward. Relational: goodness is named as a characteristic that operates in relationship — to God (divine-human goodness), to others (moral-social goodness), and to the good itself as the standard (evaluative goodness). The name implies a relational frame from the outset. Constitutional: the name implies a constitutional depth — goodness is not a behaviour but a quality of character, of inner being. This is confirmed by the Hebrew's dominant adjectival form: tov modifies the person, not merely the act. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-007) |
| OBS-067-T2-014 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Psa 119:68 | Goodness is primarily a quality — a settled characterological property of the inner being. H2896A functions predominantly as an adjective, naming what someone or something is. Agathōsunē as an abstract noun confirms this: it names the virtue, not the act. However, the registry's 12 distinct modes (OBS-067-OBS-048) reveal that goodness also operates as: a disposition (chrēstotēs — the inner orientation of generous goodwill, 886-001 and 886-002); a condition (884-009 — the inner well-being state, shalom-condition); and an evaluative act (884-007 — the divine pronouncement-of-good as a creative-constitutive act; 884-004 — the cognitive comparative assessment). The primary kind is quality, but goodness is one of the more multi-modal characteristics in the programme — it operates across quality, disposition, condition, and evaluative act depending on context and term. (OBS-067-OBS-048, OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-017) |
| OBS-067-T2-021 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Psa 119:68 | Yes — three axes of operational variation are evidenced. (1) Subject: goodness operates differently when God is subject (ontological-creative, covenantal, dispositional) vs human subject (moral-character, volitional, experiential). (2) Direction: when goodness operates God→human (divine generosity, covenantal promise, chrēstotēs) it is initiatory and enabling; when it operates human→God (praise, trust, walking with God) it is responsive; when it operates human→human (kindness, justice, instruction) it is expressive. (3) Constitutional level: agathōsunē (885-001, Volition/04) operates at the level of resolve and Spirit-enabled virtue; chrēstotēs (886-002, Moral Character/05) operates at the level of settled relational disposition; H2896A in 884-003 operates at the experiential-orientation level. The operational differences map to the 12 modes but cluster into these three axes. (OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-017, OBS-067-OBS-048) |
| OBS-067-T2-022 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Psa 119:68 | Yes — two communicative modes. (1) Doxological declaration — the refrain "for he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 100:5 etc., group 884-001) is goodness operating through corporate liturgical declaration. The speaking of goodness in praise is itself a form of its operation — it forms community, anchors trust, and reorients the inner life of the speaker toward God's character. (2) Divine revelatory speech — Mic 6:8 ("He has told you, O man, what is good") positions goodness as communicated through divine revelation. God's speaking of what is good is the mechanism by which human goodness is defined and enabled. God's good word (884-005) also operates communicatively — the covenantal word that does not fail is spoken good that reaches the inner being. The communicative mode of goodness is thus bidirectional: God speaks goodness to the human person (revelation and promise); the human person speaks goodness back to God (praise and confession). (OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-034, SBF-067-Q004) |
| OBS-067-T2-023 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 119:68 | Three immediate responses are named in the verse evidence: (1) Trust and refuge-taking — Psa 34:8 ("taste and see that the Lord is good; blessed is the man who takes refuge in him"). The immediate response to encountering divine goodness is the reflex of trust. (2) Praise and thanksgiving — 884-001 groups: the doxological refrain as immediate inner response to recognising God's goodness. (3) Receptive desire for instruction — Psa 119:68 ("you are good and do good; teach me your statutes"). The immediate response to recognising God's goodness is the desire to learn his ways — goodness encountered produces a pedagogical receptivity. These three immediate responses are consistent across the Hebrew evidence. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-026, SBF-067-Q004) |
| OBS-067-T2-031 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Psa 119:68 | The primary dimension is Moral Character (05) — this is the dominant register of H2896A in its human-subject groups (884-002: human moral character and conduct; 884-006: moral assessment of not-good; 886-002: Spirit-produced kindness as relational character quality). Moral Character covers the largest number of groups and the greatest verse count in the human register. However, given the 12 confirmed modes spanning multiple dimensions, the primary classification requires qualification: Moral Character is primary for human-subject goodness; Divine-Human Correspondence (11) is primary for divine-subject goodness (884-001, 884-007, 886-001). The registry is bipartite by dimension at the group level. (OBS-067-OBS-002, OBS-067-OBS-012, OBS-067-OBS-017, OBS-067-OBS-048) |
| OBS-067-T2-035 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Psa 119:68 | For Spirit-produced goodness (885-001, 886-002): yes — the Spirit is the originating agent. For the Hebrew H2896A doxological and covenantal groups (884-001, 884-005): the origin is God himself, whose goodness is encountered and received by the human inner being. The characteristic does not originate in the human spirit unaided — in all cases the origin is external to the person (God's character, God's Spirit, God's word). The human spirit/inner being is the site of reception, not generation. This structural finding — that goodness is not self-generated — is consistent across all 12 modes. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-039) |
| OBS-067-T2-048 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Psa 119:68 | Origin is consistently external to the person — received from God and bestowed through the Spirit. Three origin paths: (1) Divine character as origin — God is good and goodness flows from his character (Psa 119:68; 884-001); (2) Covenantal word as origin — God's good word is spoken and received in the human inner being (Jos 23:14; 884-005); (3) Spirit-production as origin — agathōsunē and chrēstotēs are Spirit-fruit (Gal 5:22; 885-001, 886-002). The key negative evidence: Rom 3:12 names the complete absence of goodness in the unregenerate condition — confirming that goodness is not naturally generated from within. The origin is not generational (no evidence for generational goodness transmission as a constitutional mechanism, though the covenantal promise "to thousands" implies relational continuity). (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-039, OBS-067-OBS-046) |
| OBS-067-T2-049 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Psa 119:68 | The origin is formally singular (God as ultimate source) but operationally multiple in its delivery mechanisms. The ultimate origin is God's own goodness — this is singular and consistent. The delivery mechanisms are: God's character encountered (experiential), God's word received (covenantal), God's Spirit infused (pneumatological). These three mechanisms are not competing origins but three modes through which the singular divine source of goodness reaches the human inner being. This is analytically significant: the programme's 12 modes of goodness all trace back to the same originating source (God's goodness) but through different delivery paths. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-039) |
| OBS-067-T2-083 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 119:68 | God-to-human operation is one of the best-evidenced dimensions of goodness across the whole registry. Four distinct modes: (1) Character declaration — God declares himself good and his goodness toward his people (Psa 119:68; 884-001 doxological group). The declaration is itself an act of divine goodness toward the human — it names what the human may trust. (2) Covenantal word and promise — 884-005 (17 verses, anchor Jos 23:14): God's good word given and kept is goodness operating from God toward the people through promise and faithfulness. (3) Creative provision — Gen 1:31 (884-007): God's pronouncement of creation as good is the foundational act of goodness-toward-creation. (4) Pneumatological gift — Gal 5:22 / Rom 15:14: the Spirit produces goodness in the believer — goodness flows from God to the human through the Spirit's infilling. (5) Chrēstotēs as divine disposition — Rom 11:22; Eph 2:7: God's generous goodwill toward humanity is named explicitly and described as displayed supremely in Christ. (OBS-067-OBS-006, OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-034, SBF-067-Q006) |
| OBS-067-T2-086 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 119:68 | Human-to-God operation is evidenced through three modes: (1) Doxological worship — the liturgical refrain "for he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (group 884-001) is goodness operating as the content of the human's praise and confession toward God. The human declares God's goodness in worship — this is goodness moving upward from the human toward God as acknowledgement and adoration. (2) Seeking and trust — Psa 34:8 ("blessed is the man who takes refuge in him") and Lam 3:25 ("the soul who seeks him"): the human's movement toward God in trust and seeking is enabled by the encounter with divine goodness. Goodness produces the movement toward God. (3) Covenantal request — Psa 119:68 ("you are good and do good; teach me your statutes"): the human addresses God's goodness as the ground for petition. God's goodness is invoked as the basis of the human's request — "because you are good, teach me." (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-033) |
| OBS-067-T2-088 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 119:68 | The human-to-God direction reveals that the relationship is one of responsive dependence grounded in prior divine initiative. The sequence is always: God's goodness is encountered or declared first → the human moves toward God in trust, praise, and seeking → God sustains and fulfils. The human's movement toward God in goodness is not autonomous spiritual achievement but a response enabled by prior encounter with divine goodness. Psa 119:68's "you are good... teach me" names the relationship structure directly: God's goodness is the basis; human receptive orientation follows. The relationship revealed is one of: initiated by God, received by the human, responded to in trust and worship, sustained in covenantal fidelity. The human's part is not passive but it is always derivative — the human's goodness-orientation toward God is itself produced by God's prior goodness toward the human. (OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-001) |
| OBS-067-T2-130 | ETYMOLOGY | Psa 119:68 | H2896A (tov) operates primarily as an adjective — modifying persons, things, words, actions, and states. Its adjectival nature means it names a quality of its subject rather than an action performed. However, H2895 (tov verbal — be pleasing) shows the root also generates a stative verb — describing the state of being good/pleasing. The grammatical range (adjective + stative verb + verbal noun in related forms) reveals that goodness in Hebrew is primarily characterological (it names what something is) and secondarily experiential (it names the state of being good/pleasing to someone). G0019 (agathōsunē) is exclusively an abstract noun — naming goodness as a substantive virtue. G5544 (chrēstotēs) is also exclusively an abstract noun. The NT's grammatical form (abstract nouns only) reveals a shift from the Hebrew's adjectival breadth (goodness applied across contexts) to a specialised inner-being virtue-noun. (OBS-067-OBS-007, OBS-067-OBS-008, SBF-067-Q088) |
| OBS-067-T2-135 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Psa 119:68 | No dedicated supplication term for goodness is present in the OWNER vocabulary. However, Lam 3:25 names "the soul who seeks him" in the context of seeking God's goodness — seeking (biqqesh / darash implied) is the posture. Psa 119:68 ("teach me your statutes") contains a petition grounded in God's goodness, functioning as a seeking-from-God. But these are not dedicated goodness-seeking terms — they are the general seeking/petition vocabulary applied in goodness contexts. A dedicated supplication form (like "ask for goodness") is absent. Partial gap. (OBS-067-OBS-033, OBS-067-OBS-026) |
| OBS-067-T2-143 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Psa 119:68 | Mic 6:8 is the primary anchor for human goodness in its most comprehensive definitional statement: "He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the Lord require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?" It functions as: (1) Revelatory — goodness is defined as divinely disclosed, not humanly generated; (2) Definitional — it names the content (justice + kindness + humility); (3) Comprehensive — it covers horizontal (justice, kindness) and vertical (humble walk with God) dimensions simultaneously; (4) Cross-registry — it is the highest-density shared anchor in the programme (6 co-anchoring registries). Psa 119:68 functions as the primary anchor for the divine-human correspondence structure of goodness: "You are good and do good" — the being/doing model at the divine level. (OBS-067-OBS-027, SBF-067-Q042, SBF-067-Q138) |
| OBS-103-T2-043 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Yes — emphatically. The commanded form of love ('you shall love' — Mat 22:37, Lev 19:18) requires the will's exercise — love cannot be merely a spontaneous feeling if it is commanded. Enemy-love (Mat 5:44, OBS-103-143) is the clearest evidence: loving enemies requires volitional engagement against natural preference. The ranking of loves (Mat 10:37, OBS-103-151) requires a deliberate choice-act — placing love of Christ above natural love of family. Ruth's chesed (Rut 3:10, OBS-103-089) — choosing the elderly Boaz over young men — is love expressed through will against self-interest. The will's engagement is not peripheral but central to love in its highest form (agapē, chesed). The affective forms (phileō, dod) may be less volitionally constituted, but the command-forms require the will. |
| OBS-103-T2-044 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Both operate in the data — love both requires and produces will-acts. It requires the will: enemy-love (Mat 5:44), ranking of loves (Mat 10:37), and Ruth's choice (Rut 3:10) are all volitional love-acts. It produces will-acts: love of Christ produces commandment-keeping (Joh 14:15, OBS-103-140) — the inner orientation generates the volitional pattern of life. The two modes are distinct: love-as-act-of-will (requiring deliberate engagement) vs love-as-generator-of-will (the inner orientation that produces volitional patterns). Both are evidenced. The affective forms (phileō, dod) may operate without explicit volitional initiation, but they too produce will-acts (Peter's fileō generates faithful mission-response, Joh 21:17, OBS-103-153). |
| OBS-103-T2-075 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Love as given outward is the primary ethical direction of the commandment structure: love your neighbour (Lev 19:18), love your enemy (Mat 5:44), love one another (Joh 13:34). The giving of love reaches its maximum in Joh 15:13 (OBS-103-125) — 'greater love has no one than this: that someone lay down his life/soul for his friends.' The gift of the soul is love's outer limit. The giving-forms span: chesed as daily covenantal faithfulness given to the covenant-partner (OBS-103-089); agapē given to the enemy against natural preference (OBS-103-143); phileō given as warmth within the community (OBS-103-119); rachamim given as compassion toward the suffering (OBS-103-105). Love-as-given is the primary ethical form in which the inner orientation is concretely actualised. |
| OBS-103-T2-083 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Yes — explicitly. 1Th 3:12 (OBS-103-130): 'may the Lord make you increase and abound in love' — love admits of increase; the Lord is the agent of its growth. The prayer-form implies that love's increase is both desirable and possible, dependent on the Lord's action. The degree-language in the data also implies growth: Rut 3:10 — 'you have made this last kindness greater than the first' — chesed measurably greater in a later act than an earlier one. Love is not fixed at a static level but admits of deepening expression and increase over time. |
| OBS-103-T2-132 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Already addressed at T5.1 (Q&A-091) and T5.1.2 (Q&A-092). Yes — 1Th 3:12 ('may the Lord make you increase and abound in love'); Rut 3:10 (this chesed greater than the first). Means: divine action (Lord makes it increase), Spirit's work (Gal 5:22), training (Tit 2:4), obedience-pathway (1Pe 1:22). No additional findings beyond Q&A-091 and Q&A-092. |
| OBS-103-T2-154 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rut 3:10 | Love **enables and extends** agency beyond its natural range. The key evidence: enemy-love (Mat 5:44) produces agency toward those the person would naturally avoid — love extends the agentive reach to include hostile others. Joh 15:13 (OBS-103-125): love produces the maximum agentive act (laying down the soul) that no other motivation could reliably sustain. Ruth's chesed-love (Rut 3:10, OBS-103-089) produces the agentive choice against self-interest — love expands agency by overriding the self-protective constraints that would otherwise limit it. Disordered love produces distorted agency: love-of-money produces the agency of departure from faith (1Ti 6:10); love-of-primacy produces the agency of exclusion (3Jo 9, OBS-103-155). The direction of love determines the direction and quality of agency — love is the inner source from which the person acts outward. |
| OBS-062-T2-160 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | All three. Directional: fellowship moves toward its object — the person joins themselves to (Hithpael reflexive) or is joined to. Relational: the characteristic is constitutively relational — it requires at minimum two parties. Constitutional: habitual fellowship constitutes the person (Jer 13:23 — fixed moral character from habitual alignment; Hos 4:17 — being "joined to idols" as a fixed spiritual condition). OBS-062-007, OBS-062-042, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-161 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Fellowship is both disposition and act — and over time becomes a settled constitutional condition. It is dispositional in Psa 119:63 ("I am a companion of all who fear you" — a declared stable orientation). It is active in Phili 3:10 (pursuing the fellowship of Christ's sufferings). It becomes constitutional in Jer 13:23 and Hos 4:17 — habitual alignment fixing into settled inner character. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052, OBS-062-042. |
| OBS-062-T2-172 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Sustained fellowship produces constitutive character formation — Jer 13:23 names the endpoint: moral character as fixed as leopard spots, immune to self-reversal. The sustained effect is not merely relational (continued connection to an object) but constitutional (the object's character is transferred into the person). Habitual companionship with God-fearers produces righteous character (Psa 119:63); habitual companionship with thieves produces corrupt character (Isa 1:23). OBS-062-052, OBS-062-048. |
| OBS-062-T2-173 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Sustained fellowship establishes: (1) Fixed moral character — aligned with the fellowship object (Jer 13:23); (2) Stable inner identity — "I am a companion of all who fear you" (Psa 119:63) as declared stable orientation; (3) In the divine mode: participation in the divine nature as a characterological state (2Pe 1:4). OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-187 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Jer 13:23 | Yes — the heart is implicated in the companionship and character-formation modes. Psa 119:63 ("I am a companion of all who fear you") is a heart-declaration: a settled orientation of the whole inner being. Jer 13:23 names fixed moral character (heart-level constitutional formation) as the product of habitual fellowship. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-188 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Jer 13:23 | Heart-location reveals fellowship as a moral-evaluative act as well as a relational one — choosing companions (Psa 119:63) is a heart-level decision that has moral character consequences (Jer 13:23). The heart that consistently fellowships with the righteous is itself formed righteous; the heart that fellowships with the corrupt is itself formed corrupt. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-219 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Fellowship directs and eventually fixes volition. Initial volitional alignment toward an object (Psa 119:63 declared companionship; Phili 3:10 aspirational pursuit) produces habitual alignment that over time becomes constitutional (Jer 13:23 — fixed character). The volitional act of self-joining (Hithpael) is the beginning; constitutional fixing is the endpoint. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052, OBS-062-055. |
| OBS-062-T2-220 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Volition is both the initiating act of fellowship and its progressive captive — the will that begins by choosing companions is eventually formed by them such that alternative choices become progressively harder (Jer 13:23 — "can the leopard change its spots?"). OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-222 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Fellowship initially engages agency and then — in the constitutional mode — transforms it. Habitual fellowship with God-fearers produces a person who naturally orients toward the righteous (agency reshaped). Habitual wrong-fellowship produces a person who can no longer choose differently (agency progressively constrained by constitutional formation — Jer 13:23). OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-254 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Both. Condition: fellowship transforms the inner condition from corruption (2Pe 1:4 — escape from corruption) to divine nature participation. Orientation: habitual fellowship fixes the person's stable orientation toward their fellowship objects (Jer 13:23 — character as fixed as leopard spots). OBS-062-058, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-255 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Constitutional formation from habitual fellowship is presented as difficult to reverse — Jer 13:23 ("can the leopard change its spots?") implies that deep formation becomes resistant to change. Whether this is fully irreversible is not stated, but the formative direction is resistant to simple volitional reversal. OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-062-T2-256 | VERSE_PATTERN | Jer 13:23 | Yes — a formation sequence: (1) Volitional alignment / self-joining (Psa 119:63, Phili 3:10 — initiating acts); (2) Habitual participation (sustained fellowship over time); (3) Constitutional formation (Jer 13:23 — fixed character as the endpoint). For divine nature participation (2Pe 1:4): (1) Receipt of divine promise; (2) Escape from corrupting desire; (3) Participation in divine nature. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-258 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Two distinct mechanisms: (1) Participatory transfer — the person participates in what the fellowship object is, and through sustained participation, is formed into conformity with it (Psa 119:63 → Jer 13:23 line; 2Pe 1:4). (2) Wound-as-cleansing — external physical event (Pro 20:30 — strokes) producing internal purification (cleaning of innermost parts). Mechanism 1 operates through habitual relational alignment; Mechanism 2 operates through somatic event with inner-being consequence. OBS-062-019, OBS-062-039. |
| OBS-062-T2-262 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Yes — habitual fellowship is the primary mechanism of character formation in this registry. The companionship-habituation arc (Psa 119:63 → Jer 13:23) is a sanctification arc: sustained alignment with God-fearers forms righteous character. 2Pe 1:4's divine nature participation is the telos of the fellowship-sanctification arc. OBS-062-048, OBS-062-052, OBS-062-058. |
| OBS-062-T2-304 | OBSERVATION | Jer 13:23 | Social psychology surfaces the question of fellowship's role in moral formation when the fellowship community itself is corrupted — the echo chamber dynamic, where wrong fellowship degrades the very faculties needed to recognise the error (consistent with Jer 13:23, Hos 4:17). This dimension is present in the biblical data but not named as systematically as human science frameworks would. OBS-062-042, OBS-062-052. |
| OBS-099-T2-006 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Kindness instantiates the outward-oriented relational disposition of God toward others. Mic 6:8 and Eph 4:32 together reveal that the relational-moral complex (justice, kindness, humility; kindness, tenderheartedness, forgiveness) is what the image-bearer embodies [OBS-099-052, OBS-099-077]. The specific divine likeness instantiated: the freedom and generosity to act in favour of another without compulsion — Ruth's kindness to Naomi (Rut 3:10 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]) is the clearest human illustration. |
| OBS-099-T2-007 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Genuinely shared, with structural asymmetry. The same terms (chesed, chrēstos/chrēstotēs) are predicated of both without distinction of kind. 2Sa 22:26 makes the sharing explicit: "With the merciful you show yourself merciful" [XREG: H2616A, OBS-099-096]. Asymmetry: God's chesed is inexhaustible and eschatologically guaranteed; human chesed is creaturely and can be withdrawn (Hos 6:6 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]). The sharing is real; the asymmetry is structural. |
| OBS-099-T2-009 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Yes — three typological movements: (a) Covenantal: each individual divine chesed act participates in and points toward the one covenantal faithfulness underlying them all [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]. (b) Christological: Eph 2:7 names Christ as the supreme display of divine kindness — the eschatological revelation of what chesed has always been pointing toward [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. (c) Human-divine mirror: Ruth's kindness, 1Sa 20:14, 2Sa 10:2 — each human chesed act is typological of the divine pattern [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-070/071]. |
| OBS-099-T2-010 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Primary direction: divine-to-human. Eph 4:32 — "be kind... as God in Christ forgave you" [OBS-099-077]. The divine instance establishes the pattern; human kindness is the derived expression. Secondary direction: human-pointing-to-divine — each human chesed act points toward the divine kindness it participates in. Both directions present; primary movement is from divine instance to human response. |
| OBS-099-T2-016 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Working description: Kindness is a stable inner disposition of loyal goodwill toward another, rooted in the heart, that orients the whole person toward the practical benefit and relational flourishing of its object, regardless of desert. Constituted by faithfulness (holds across time and through failure), compassion (feels with the other), and absence of self-serving motivation. In divine form: inexhaustible, unconditional in ultimate terms, purposively aimed at transformation. In human form: creaturely, finite, Spirit-formed, called to mirror the divine pattern. Primary constitutional location: heart. Primary expression: deliberate relational action. Counterfeit: pleasant speech covering self-serving motivation (chrēstologia). |
| OBS-099-T2-020 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Three modes: (a) Dispositional orientation — chasid is characterised by this as settled personal quality [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. Prior to specific acts; the inner ground from which acts arise. (b) Deliberate act of will — Ruth's graduated kindness, David's covenant-loyalty, Judah's surety-pledge — each a chosen act [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-071, OBS-099-063]. (c) Spirit's interior work — Gal 5:22 — chrēstotēs as fruit of the Spirit; formation mode in which kindness is produced organically [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-092]. Three modes are not mutually exclusive: Spirit forms the disposition; disposition generates deliberate acts. |
| OBS-099-T2-031 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Primary dimension: Relational Disposition (06). Kindness is fundamentally an orientation-toward-another — stable inner stance directed outward toward a relational partner. The chesed vocabulary is constitutively relational: it exists within and sustains covenant. Eight of 27 OWNER groups carry Relational Disposition — largest single dimension. H2617A group 536-001 (180 relevant verses, dominant subject GOD [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083]) confirms at scale. |
| OBS-099-T2-032 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Multi-stranded: (a) Chesed appears overwhelmingly in covenant contexts [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]. (b) All acts of kindness in the data are directed toward specific others [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-063, OBS-099-071, OBS-099-060]. (c) The structural opposite (pride, self-enclosure) is a relational failure [OBS-099-054]. (d) The counterfeit (chrēstologia) fails precisely in the relational direction [OBS-099-074]. Relational Disposition is the correct primary dimension. |
| OBS-099-T2-045 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Yes — two-level movement: (a) Spirit → Soul/Heart: Gal 5:22 (Spirit produces) → Pro 3:3 (written on heart) — Spirit-formation produces heart-level disposition [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-092; XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]. (b) Heart → Relational Action: Pro 3:3 disposition → Rut 3:10 / 2Sa 10:2 / Gen 44:32 — deliberate acts toward others [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-071, OBS-099-063]. Full constitutional movement: Spirit-formation → heart-inscription → relational disposition → deliberate acts. Coherent downward-outward movement from deep to surface, inner to relational. |
| OBS-099-T2-049 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Yes — strongly. (a) Deliberate choice: Rut 3:10 — Ruth's kindness greater than the first — a choice when she did not have to [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]; 2Sa 10:2 — David resolved to show loyalty [OBS-099-071]; Gen 44:32 — Judah placed himself under obligation by choice [OBS-099-063]. (b) Love as volitional act: Mic 6:8 — "love kindness" — love in OT covenant context is a volitional commitment [OBS-099-052]. (c) Putting on: Col 3:12 — "put on... kindness" — deliberate clothing [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Kindness strongly engages the will; it is chosen, not merely felt. |
| OBS-099-T2-053 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Yes — conscientiousness (integrated moral awareness + volition + action) is precisely what kindness requires and produces. The chasid — moral awareness (chesed is right), volition (I will show chesed), action (I do show chesed) integrated into consistent reliable character [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. Pro 3:3 — "let not steadfast love forsake you" — ongoing conscientiousness required [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]. Ruth's graduated kindness shows conscientiousness across multiple acts [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]. Kindness and conscientiousness mutually reinforcing. |
| OBS-099-T2-057 | OBSERVATION | Rut 3:10 | Well-evidenced. (a) How: specific deliberate acts — Ruth to Naomi (Rut 3:10 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-086]); David to Hanun (2Sa 10:2 [OBS-099-071]); Jonathan/David (1Sa 20:14 [OBS-099-070]); Judah's surety-pledge (Gen 44:32 [OBS-099-063]). Each concrete, costly, relational. (b) Inner conditions in giver: the chasid — settled inner character [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]; heart-inscription prior to the act (Pro 3:3 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]). (c) What the person must have received: Eph 4:32 — "as God in Christ forgave you, be kind" [OBS-099-077]. Grounded in having received divine kindness/forgiveness first. |

(none)

<a id="t-G5485"></a>
### G5485 charis — grace (`mti_id=888`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G5485` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R068 grace · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: —
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 241
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=888` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`
6. Mounce short-def: grace, the state of kindness and favor toward someone, often with a focus on a benefit given to the object; by extension: gift, benefit; credit; words of kindness and benefit: thanks, blessing

**Verses:** 88 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (4)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1121 | 888-001 | Term names grace as the sovereign, unmerited divine disposition toward humanity — the freely given, transforming gift through which God justifies, saves, calls, and sustains, supremely displayed in Christ |  |
| 1122 | 888-002 | Term names grace as the relational sphere and power in which the believer stands and lives — the ongoing inner-relational condition of being under grace, accessed by faith, sustained by God, and expressed in the transformed inner being |  |
| 1123 | 888-003 | Term names grace as relational favour — the disposition of goodwill between persons or between a person and God, which opens access, creates loyalty, and reflects the character of the one granting it |  |
| 1124 | 888-004 | Term names grace as the animating power and sphere of apostolic mission — the word of grace, the commendation to grace, the extending of grace to more people, and the inner source of ministry identity |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (5)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-068-T2-002 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — extensively. G5485 charis in group 888-001 names grace as "sovereign, unmerited divine disposition." H2580 chen in 889-001 names "divine relational favour." G5487 charitoō (Luk 1:28 anchor) names God's act of bestowing grace as a completed act that transforms the recipient's standing. The attribution reveals that when grace appears in the human person — as character quality (889-003), as forgiving act (5470-002), as communicative quality (SD022) — it is always derivative: the human expression of grace is a secondary reflection of the divine original. *Consistent with prior analysis. Adds structure: the derivative character of human grace is made explicit as a T0-level claim.* |
| OBS-068-T2-012 | OBSERVATION |  | H2580 chen (favour) signals relational standing — being seen with favour by one who has power to grant or withhold. H8469 tachanum (supplication) signals the receiving side — the posture of the creature appealing for what it cannot command. G5485 charis signals the full semantic arc. Together the primary terms reveal a two-faced definitional structure: the disposition of the giver (chen, charis) and the posture of the receiver (tachanum). The vocabulary embeds the relational architecture before analysis begins: grace requires both a giver and a receiver oriented toward each other. *Adds structure: the two-faced definitional structure (giver/receiver) as a T1-level observation — the vocabulary encodes the relational architecture at first encounter.* |
| OBS-068-T2-121 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes. charis/chairo (G5485/G5463) root-sharing with rejoicing (SD020): characteristics sharing the charis root share a conceptual relationship to free, joyful giving. CHEN root family (SD024): the most complete root architecture in the OT vocabulary, spanning the full grace-circuit. *Consistent with prior analysis (SD020, SD024). Adds T6 framing.* |
| OBS-068-T2-130 | OBSERVATION |  | H2580 chen (favour — root: to bend toward, to show partiality; the disposition of the greater who inclines toward the lesser). H8469 tachanum (supplication — root shared with chen: the appeal of the creature for grace). G5485 charis (grace — root χαρ, shared with chairo — to rejoice: grace as joyful giving). Root meanings reveal: grace is constitutively an inclination of the greater toward the lesser (chen root); inherently joyful in character (charis/chairo); the receiving-side vocabulary (tachanum) shares the same root as the giving-side (chen/chanan) — the vocabulary embeds the two-sided structure in a single root family. *Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T7 framing.* |
| OBS-068-T2-133 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — unusually completely. Disposition: chen (H2580), charis (G5485) as noun. Act: chanan (H2603A), charizō (G5483). Completed state/condition: charitoō (G5487) as perfect passive. Quality: chen in 889-003, charis in speech contexts. Received side: tachanum (H8469). Given side: chanan, charizō. The vocabulary is structurally complete — every dimension of grace's operation has a corresponding term. *Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T7 framing: structural completeness of vocabulary is a significant lexical finding.* |

**Findings — via anchor verse (32 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-039-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Eph 2:8 | Group 4986-001 in debauchery (#39, C12) contains 57 verses of salvation vocabulary (sōzō/sōtēria), anchored at Eph 2:8 ('by grace you have been saved through faith'). Related verses span the NT salvation corpus: Mat 10:22, 19:25, 24:13, Mar 16:16, Luk 8:12, Joh 3:17, Act 2:21, 4:12, and more. This group is structurally unexpected in the debauchery registry but was placed there on the basis of the verse evidence — salvation vocabulary appears in debauchery-adjacent contexts. Session B analyst should assess the connection: is salvation specifically named as the remedy for debauchery/licentiousness in these contexts, does the term appear because the debauchery file included a broad NT lexical sweep, or is there a distinct theological pattern linking salvation and debauchery vocabulary in Scripture? The answer has implications for how the inner-being dimension of salvation is characterised within this registry. This is also a Session D synthesis candidate — the relationship between salvation and debauchery vocabulary across the programme. |
| DIM-29-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | 2Cor 9:8 | Both contentment terms name a stable inner condition independent of circumstances — a learned/acquired disposition of sufficiency, not a transient emotional state. Session B should examine whether contentment constitutes a distinct sub-dimension of Character/Disposition, and how it relates to the Philippians 4 context where it appears alongside joy and peace. |
| DIM-97-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | 2Cor 12:9 | Three groups name joy that persists through or is intensified by suffering: 364-001 (salad — defiant exultation), 381-002 (chairo — paradoxical joy in tribulation), and 3859-001 in Registry 42 (hēdista — most gladly in weakness). This convergent cross-term pattern names a distinct inner-being mode. Session B should examine joy-through-suffering as a structural feature rather than an anomaly. |
| OBS-067-T2-002 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | Scripture attributes goodness to God extensively and explicitly. Psa 119:68 ("You are good"), Psa 34:8 ("the Lord is good"), Psa 100:5 and Ps 106:1 / 107:1 / 118:1 (doxological refrain "for he is good"), Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him"), Rom 11:22 (God's chrēstotēs), Eph 2:7 (God's chrēstotēs in Christ). The attribution reveals that goodness in the human person is not an independent achievement but a participation in and reflection of an attribute that originates in God. Human goodness is derivative by design — it is what being made in the image of the good God looks like in a creature. This has direct significance: the human person who embodies goodness is expressing their created nature; the human person who fails to do so is in a condition of departure from original design. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-006) |
| OBS-067-T2-005 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | The evidence is suggestive but not fully explicit. 2Th 1:11 ("may God fulfil every resolve for good and every work of faith by his power") implies an eschatological completeness — the resolve for good is present now, but its fulfilment is anticipated as God's completing act. Eph 2:7 places God's display of chrēstotēs "in the coming ages" — goodness has a future-facing dimension of fullness. Gal 5:22 as Spirit-fruit connects goodness to the present work of the Spirit that anticipates the fullness of Spirit-transformation. However, the data does not contain a verse that explicitly narrates goodness as moving toward eschatological fullness in the way that glory or resurrection does. The eschatological trajectory is implied by the pneumatological framework (Spirit-production → Spirit-completion) but not explicitly named. This is a partial gap. (OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016) |
| OBS-067-T2-009 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | The covenantal typological dimension is present and clear: God's good word (884-005, Jos 23:14) and his chrēstotēs displayed supremely in Christ (Eph 2:7) both carry a Christological register — the fullness of divine goodness is displayed in Christ's person and work. Rom 2:4 names chrēstotēs as the mechanism through which God leads to repentance, which is structurally a Christological movement. However, the data does not contain an explicit typological argument (e.g., the good king who typifies the messianic king, or a creation-goodness → new-creation-goodness typology). The christological and eschatological dimensions are present in NT usage but not through formal typological structure. This is a partial finding — the direction is named but the full typological structure is not explicitly drawn in the verse evidence. (OBS-067-OBS-006, OBS-067-OBS-036) |
| OBS-067-T2-085 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | God's extension of goodness reveals a disposition of initiatory, generous, and sustaining goodwill toward the human person. Three dimensions: (1) Initiatory — God acts first in goodness (Gen 1:31; Eph 2:7 — "so that in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus"). The initiative is always divine. (2) Generous — chrēstotēs names not merely adequate goodness but generous goodwill (886-001): God's disposition is one of overflowing benevolence. (3) Sustaining — the covenantal promise (884-005, Jos 23:14: "not one word has failed") names a goodness that holds through time and maintains its commitment. The disposition is not reactive but proactive, generous, and reliable. Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him") names waiting as the human posture corresponding to this divine sustaining goodness — the relationship is not passive but involves a human receptive posture. (OBS-067-OBS-006, OBS-067-OBS-033, OBS-067-OBS-034, SBF-067-Q006) |
| OBS-067-T2-109 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | Partial eschatological evidence is present. Eph 2:7 ("so that in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus") explicitly places chrēstotēs in a future-oriented eschatological frame — God's chrēstotēs will be displayed in its fullness in the coming ages. This names an eschatological trajectory: present experience of divine kindness anticipates its future unrestricted display. 2Th 1:11 ("fulfil every resolve for good") implies a completion — God's completing act brings present goodness-resolve to its fullness. However, the specifically eschatological texture of goodness (what it will be in the new creation, what fullness means) is not directly described in the anchor verses beyond these two instances. The trajectory is indicated but not fully developed. (OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016, SBF-067-Q006) |
| OBS-067-T2-110 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 2:7 | Eph 2:7 implies that present experience of chrēstotēs is a foretaste of the "immeasurable riches" of its future expression — present goodness is real but partial relative to its coming fullness. The "immeasurable" qualifier suggests that the eschatological form of goodness exceeds present experience in scale and richness. 2Th 1:11's "fulfil every resolve for good" implies that present resolves for goodness, even when genuine, are not yet complete — their completion is a divine eschatological act. The present experience of goodness therefore anticipates: (1) A fullness of divine kindness-display that surpasses present experience; (2) A completion of human goodness-resolve that requires divine power to finish. The gap is that the data does not describe what the experiential content of eschatological goodness will be — only that it will be fuller and completed. (OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-016) |
| OBS-103-T2-014 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:7 | Love in the data is simultaneously a disposition (stable inner orientation toward), an act (commanded, exercised, expressed — agapaō is consistently verbal), and a condition (one who is loved — agapētos — is in a constitutive relational state). It is not primarily an emotion, though it produces emotional states (joy, grief, rest). The commanded form (Mat 22:37 — 'you shall love') establishes love as an act of the will, not purely a felt state. The dispositional form (chesed as God's defining attribute, OBS-103-025) establishes love as a stable character quality. The received form (agapētos as identity, OBS-103-040) establishes love as a constitutive condition the person exists within. Love is therefore tripartite: disposition + act + condition — inner orientation that produces acts and exists within a condition of being-loved. This is the most structurally complex inner-being phenomenon in the programme. |
| OBS-103-T2-026 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:7 | From the data: sustained possession of love establishes — (a) **identity as beloved** (agapētos — the one constituted by divine love, OBS-103-040, OBS-103-122): sustained divine love toward the person establishes the identity of being-loved; (b) **relational capacity** — sustained love of neighbour establishes the inner orientation of fairness, care, and honesty that Lev 19 describes (OBS-103-071); (c) **community legibility** — the community constituted by mutual love becomes publicly readable (Joh 13:35, OBS-103-129); (d) **faith-pattern** — sustained love of God produces sustained commandment-keeping (Joh 14:15, OBS-103-140); (e) **soul-satisfaction** — sustained experience of God's goodness/love satisfies the soul's deepest hunger (Jer 31:14, OBS-103-081). The overarching capacity established: the person becomes capable of giving what they have received — sustained reception of divine love equips the person to love others. |
| OBS-103-T2-067 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:7 | This is one of love's most significant contributions in the data. Belovedness as identity: agapētos (OBS-103-040, OBS-103-122) — the believer's primary identity designation is 'loved by God and called.' Being loved constitutes who the person is; identity is received through the love-relationship, not self-generated. Mat 3:17 (OBS-103-120): the Father's declaration 'this is my beloved Son' constitutes Christ's identity — being named beloved is identity-conferral. Joh 13:35 (OBS-103-129): the community's identity ('my disciples') is constituted by its love for one another — what you love constitutes your community identity. What you love as the individual also constitutes personal identity: the money-lover (OBS-103-132), the God-lover (OBS-103-118), the world-lover (OBS-103-148) — love is the diagnostic identity-marker. Identity in the biblical data is constituted by: (a) being loved (passive — agapētos) and (b) what you love (active — the love-object as identity-definer). |
| OBS-103-T2-071 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:7 | The receiver is constituted as agapētos (beloved) — an identity state, not merely a felt experience (OBS-103-040, OBS-103-122). The immediate inner-being state upon receiving divine love: rest/quieting (Zep 3:17, OBS-103-094); fullness and pleasure (Psa 16:11, OBS-103-112); soul-satisfaction (Jer 31:14, OBS-103-081). The sustained inner-being state: security ('I will never leave you' — Heb 13:5), known-ness ('Lord, you know everything — Joh 21:17, OBS-103-153), and free-from-striving (Psa 127:2, OBS-103-084). The person who receives divine love becomes the person who rests in it. The identity-state (beloved) and the experiential state (rest, fullness) are both dimensions of the receiver's inner condition. |
| OBS-103-T2-076 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:7 | Love as received is constitutive of identity (agapētos, OBS-103-040), productive of rest (Zep 3:17, OBS-103-094), and the enabling condition for giving love (1Jo 4:19, OBS-103-146). The reception is: (a) constitutive — being named beloved by God constitutes who the person is; (b) enabling — divine love received is what makes human love possible (1Jo 4:19); (c) experiential — rest, fullness, and joy are the experiential register of received love. Reception of love is not passive — Song 5:2 (OBS-103-108) shows the beloved opening the door; Luk 15:20 (OBS-103-057) shows the son returning. Some movement toward the source appears to be the condition for reception (Q&A-031). |
| OBS-103-T2-110 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:7 | Taking agapaō/agapē as the primary NT term: **agapaō** (verb, 344 occurrences) — love as an action, exercisable and commandable; the verbal form affirms love's volitional dimension. **agapē** (noun, 133 occurrences) — love as an entity, a reality that can be 'poured into hearts' (Rom 5:5), that 'never fails' (1Cor 13:8), that is the greatest (1Cor 13:13). **agapētos** (adjective/participle, 79 occurrences) — beloved, the passive recipient of love; love as constituting an identity-state in the object. Together: verb + noun + adjective-participle spans the full grammatical range: love as act (verb), love as reality/quality (noun), and love as identity-constitutive state (adjective). This three-part grammatical range confirms Q&A-016's finding that love is simultaneously act, disposition, and condition. For chesed: primarily noun (covenant-quality) with verbal cognate (chasad — to act with chesed) — the noun-primary form confirms love as a stable quality before it is an act. |
| OBS-062-T2-149 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 2Cor 13:14 | Yes — God is described as the one who joins (H2266 Hiphil — Eze 37:19: "I will join with it the stick of Judah"). God initiates union and fellowship as active agent. The fellowship of the Holy Spirit (2Cor 13:14) attributes fellowship to the Spirit as a divine act. Attribution reveals: God is not a passive object of human fellowship-seeking but the active initiator who joins himself to and through his people. OBS-062-051, OBS-062-030. |
| OBS-062-T2-181 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | 2Cor 13:14 | The spirit (*ruach*) is not explicitly named as the locus of fellowship in the primary anchor verses. The closest: 2Cor 13:14 ("the fellowship of the Holy Spirit") places the Spirit as the medium or agent of fellowship, not the human spirit as locus. Phili 3:10's "becoming like him in his death" implies spirit-level transformation through fellowship-in-suffering. Partially evidenced. OBS-062-055, OBS-062-030. |
| OBS-062-T2-236 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 2Cor 13:14 | Yes — divine initiative is the origin of the highest fellowship modes. God joins (Eze 37:19 — divine action). God promises divine nature participation (2Pe 1:4). The Spirit mediates fellowship (2Cor 13:14). OBS-062-051, OBS-062-058, OBS-062-030. |
| OBS-062-T2-253 | OBSERVATION | 2Cor 13:14 | 2Cor 13:14 ("the fellowship of the Holy Spirit") presents the Spirit as the mediator of fellowship — the divine Spirit strengthens and enables fellowship rather than merely witnessing it. This is the nearest evidence of a spiritual being strengthening fellowship. OBS-062-030. |
| OBS-064-T2-149 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 1:7 | Yes — the salach root family deploys typologically. The OT cultic atonement-forgiveness (H5545 — "they shall be forgiven") operates typologically toward the NT's atonement through Christ's blood (Eph 1:7 — "redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses"). The sacrificial system typologically anticipates the definitive atonement. Isa 53:5 (shared anchor with R030 contrition) extends this: the Servant's wounds produce peace and healing — the typological mechanism is suffering-as-substitution producing release-of-debt. OBS-064-014, OBS-064-028. |
| OBS-064-T2-150 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 1:7 | Typological direction: from cultic-formulaic (OT sacrificial system — external mechanism producing declared forgiveness) to personal-atoning (Christ's blood as the definitive sacrifice — producing actual forgiveness of trespasses, Col 2:13 — "having forgiven us all our trespasses"). The typology moves from mechanism to its fulfilment, from shadow to substance. OBS-064-014, OBS-064-028. |
| OBS-064-T2-189 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | Eph 1:7 | The direction runs from somatic compassion inward and upward to the volitional act (Mat 18:27 — somatic response → forgiveness decision). In the atonement mechanism, the direction runs from somatic suffering outward to released debt-status in others (Isa 53:5; Eph 1:7). OBS-064-022, OBS-064-028. |
| OBS-064-T2-226 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 1:7 | Yes — the primary and dominant relational direction in this registry. God is the exclusive subject of H5545 (salach). The NT forgiveness pronouncements (Luk 7:47-48, Mar 2:5) are divine-to-human. Eph 1:7 ("through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses") names the divine mechanism. Col 2:13 ("having forgiven us all our trespasses") — divine agentive act. OBS-064-003, OBS-064-022. |
| OBS-064-T2-227 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Eph 1:7 | God extends forgiveness on the basis of divine character and atoning mechanism, not human merit. Dan 9:19 ("for your own sake") grounds the intercessory plea entirely in God's own character. Eph 1:7 grounds forgiveness in Christ's blood — the divine provision, not human performance. The access-postures (faith, repentance, confession) are receptive conditions, not earning conditions. OBS-064-016, OBS-064-022. |
| OBS-064-T2-239 | OBSERVATION | Eph 1:7 | The primary forgiveness scope is covenantal — within the covenant relationship with YHWH (OT) or through Christ (NT, Eph 1:7). The NT extends the scope through the universal offer — Act 10:43 ("everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins") — making the covenantal access theoretically universal. The scope is not ethnically restricted in the NT. OBS-064-022, OBS-064-028. |
| OBS-064-T2-248 | OBSERVATION | Eph 1:7 | Two mechanisms: (1) Declaratory mechanism — the forgiveness pronouncement effects a status change. God's declaration ("your sins are forgiven") or the human's inner release creates the new condition. (2) Atoning mechanism — the blood of Christ (Eph 1:7) is the structural ground that enables the declaration. The atonement mechanism is the basis; the declaration is the application. OBS-064-022, OBS-064-028. |
| OBS-064-T2-250 | OBSERVATION | Eph 1:7 | Yes — forgiveness is structurally linked to suffering. The atoning mechanism (Eph 1:7 — "through his blood"; Heb 9:22 — "without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness") grounds forgiveness in suffering on the forgiver's side. The somatic compassion (splagchnizomai — Mat 18:27) is a suffered response in the forgiver's body. OBS-064-022, OBS-064-028. |
| OBS-064-T2-251 | OBSERVATION | Eph 1:7 | Suffering is the cost-side of forgiveness on the forgiver's part. The divine cost is the highest: the death of Christ (Eph 1:7). The human cost is the absorbed wrong: the forgiver absorbs the cost of the offence rather than extracting it. Suffering does not produce forgiveness mechanically but is structurally necessary to it — the release of the claim always involves the claim-holder absorbing what was owed. OBS-064-022, OBS-064-028. |
| OBS-099-T2-005 | OBSERVATION | Eph 2:7 | Yes. Eph 2:7 places divine kindness in an eschatological frame: "in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus" [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Rom 2:4 establishes that divine kindness is purposively aimed at repentance — directed toward a future state not yet present [OBS-099-076]. Kindness has an eschatological trajectory: both in the recipient (transformation) and in the divine economy (immeasurable future display). |
| OBS-099-T2-009 | OBSERVATION | Eph 2:7 | Yes — three typological movements: (a) Covenantal: each individual divine chesed act participates in and points toward the one covenantal faithfulness underlying them all [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]. (b) Christological: Eph 2:7 names Christ as the supreme display of divine kindness — the eschatological revelation of what chesed has always been pointing toward [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. (c) Human-divine mirror: Ruth's kindness, 1Sa 20:14, 2Sa 10:2 — each human chesed act is typological of the divine pattern [OBS-099-086, OBS-099-070/071]. |
| OBS-099-T2-065 | OBSERVATION | Eph 2:7 | Yes — three eschatological markers: (a) Eschatological escalation: Eph 2:7 — "in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus" [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Future form exceeds present expression. (b) Preservation of the chasid: Psa 16:10 — "you will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption" [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-095]. Chesed-shaped person eschatologically preserved; formation survives death. (c) Telos: Rom 2:4 (repentance) and Eph 2:7 (eschatological display) together — divine kindness is purposive from beginning to end. |
| OBS-099-T2-070 | OBSERVATION | Eph 2:7 | Partial evidence for three boundaries: (a) Kindness vs. love: 1Cor 13:4 — kindness is a mode of love's operation, not its definition [OBS-099-072, Q&A-021]. Love is broader; kindness is the specific outward-oriented gracious-action mode. (b) Kindness vs. compassion: Eph 4:32 — kindness and tenderheartedness are distinct qualities [OBS-099-077]. Compassion feels; kindness acts from that feeling. (c) Kindness vs. grace: Eph 2:7 — grace names the favour; kindness names the disposition in which the favour is given [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Gap: boundaries implied by co-occurrence and context but not explicitly theorised. Session D needed for full boundary analysis. |

**SD pointers — direct (2)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-068-SD020 | HIGH |  | charis and chairo (to rejoice, be glad) share the lexical root χαρ-. Grace and joy are not merely theologically related — they share a root. Grace is the disposition of one who gives with gladness; receiving grace produces joy as its natural inner response. G5485 (charis) appears as the one shared xref term with rejoicing (Reg 132). For Session D: is there a structural inner-being relationship between grace and joy that the root reveals — grace as the ground of joy, joy as the inner-being response to grace? Does the rejoicing registry encode this root-level connection? |
| DIM-068-SD043 | MEDIUM |  | G0884 acharistos (ungrateful, graceless — related word of G5483/G5485) is the structural antithesis of grace lexically encoded in the same root family. Ingratitude is the inner failure to recognise grace as grace — receiving the gift without registering it as gift. For Session D: does the gratitude registry (Reg 69, cooccurrence 3 verses) and the pride registry (Reg 123, cooccurrence 3 verses + shared anchor) converge on acharistos as the inner condition most opposed to grace-reception? Is ingratitude — the inner failure to register grace as grace — the same inner-being condition as pride — the inner elevation of the self that excludes the logic of gift? |

<a id="t-H2603A"></a>
### H2603A cha.nan — be gracious (`mti_id=984`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H2603A` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R111 mercy · Legacy C-cluster: C17
3. Sub-group: —
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 81
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=984` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`

**Verses:** 72 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1641 | 984-001 | Term names the act of being gracious or showing favour — God's inner disposition of grace toward the supplicant, and the human cry that turns to God in petition and dependence |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (6)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-068-T2-133 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — unusually completely. Disposition: chen (H2580), charis (G5485) as noun. Act: chanan (H2603A), charizō (G5483). Completed state/condition: charitoō (G5487) as perfect passive. Quality: chen in 889-003, charis in speech contexts. Received side: tachanum (H8469). Given side: chanan, charizō. The vocabulary is structurally complete — every dimension of grace's operation has a corresponding term. *Consistent with prior analysis. Adds T7 framing: structural completeness of vocabulary is a significant lexical finding.* |
| OBS-023-T2-060 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — the data reveals a specific and significant engagement with memory, operating in two directions. **Memory as the appeal ground in supplication:** The supplication vocabulary (H8467 techinah, H8469 tahanun, H2603A chanan as XREF) names the inner act of earnest appeal to God for compassion. Psa 25:7 (group 1633-003): "Remember not the sins of my youth or my transgressions; according to your steadfast love remember me, for the sake of your goodness, O Lord!" The petition explicitly asks God to remember the person through the lens of chesed rather than through the lens of the person's sin-history. This is a direct engagement with God's memory as the ground of compassion's extension: the appeal asks that divine memory selectively retrieve the covenantal loyalty rather than the moral record. **God's remembering as compassion-act:** The inverse is also present — God remembering with compassion is a specific inner act. Psa 25:7 treats divine memory as something that can be oriented differently depending on whether it operates through chesed or through the record of transgressions. This makes memory a structural component of compassion's operation in the divine-human relationship. **Memory as the context of compassion's sustaining:** Psa 48:9 (group 1633-003): "We have thought on your steadfast love, O God, in the midst of your temple." Meditating on God's chesed is named as a sustained inner act — holding the memory of divine compassion-love in the mind as an orienting reality. This is memory in service of compassion's sustaining effect: remembering the divine compassion shapes the inner life of the one who remembers. |
| OBS-023-T2-122 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — the registry header confirms: shared_term_count = 56, term_sharing_ratio = 0.577. Of the 97 total terms, 56 (57.7%) are shared with other registries. This is a very high sharing ratio, reflecting the structural position of compassion within a dense cluster of adjacent characteristics. Key XREF terms (shared vocabulary carried in other registries as OWNER): - G1653 eleeō / G1656 eleos / G3628 oiktirmos — mercy (R111) - H2617A chesed — love/kindness (R103/R099) - H2603A chanan — grace (R68) - H2623 chasid — devotion/loyalty (R46/R104) - H7349 rachum / H7355 racham / H7356B rachamim — also in love (R103) - H5162G/H nacham — comfort (R192) - H8467 techinah / H8469 tahanun — prayer/supplication (R212/R122) The breadth of vocabulary sharing confirms that compassion occupies a structurally central position in the programme — its vocabulary reaches across multiple clusters including C17 (love, mercy, kindness), C14 (will, surrender), prayer/supplication registries, and the comfort cluster. |
| OBS-023-T2-139 | OBSERVATION |  | Yes — the supplication vocabulary is a substantive and structurally significant XREF set in the data: **H8467 techinah** (XREF, 24 occurrences): "supplication/plea for favour" — the earnest appeal for divine compassion and grace. Derived from the CHANAN root. **H8469 tahanun** (XREF, 18 occurrences): "entreaty/supplication" — closely related; names the act of earnest pleading for divine mercy. **H2603A chanan** (XREF, 72 occurrences): "be gracious" — the verbal form often rendered "have mercy" in contexts of prayer. The imperative form (chonneni — "be gracious to me") is the prayer-appeal for divine compassion. **H5165 nech.amah / H5164 no.cham** (XREF, 2/1 occurrences): seeking comfort/compassion from God in the context of grief. The supplication vocabulary establishes that compassion has a well-developed seeking-language — the human person's acts of appeal to God for compassion are named and differentiated in the vocabulary. This is not merely incidental: the existence of a supplication sub-vocabulary confirms that compassion is understood as something that can be sought, approached, and appealed for — it is not merely received passively but actively sought in earnest petition. |
| OBS-111-T2-134 | ETYMOLOGY | N/A | Primary terms and roots: (1) H2603A *chanan* — to be gracious, to bend toward the lesser; root implies condescension of the greater to the need of the lesser. (2) H3722A *kipper* — to cover, to wipe out, to atone; root *kaphar* means to cover or smear over — the covering of guilt. (3) H3727 *kapporet* — from *kaphar* root; the covering-place, mercy seat. (4) G1656 *eleos* — mercy, compassion; from *hilaos* (propitious/gracious). (5) G3628 *oiktirmos* — compassion from the viscera; gut-level mercy. (6) H7356B *rachamim* — compassion; from *rechem* (womb) — mercy as womb-generative care for one's own offspring. Roots reveal: mercy spans condescension (chanan), covering (kipper), gut-compassion (oiktirmos), and womb-generativity (rachamim). OBS-111-008, OBS-111-009, OBS-111-010, OBS-111-014. |
| OBS-111-T2-135 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Mat 5:7, Mat 18:33, Rom 9:15 | For *eleos* (G1656, the dominant Greek noun, 26 active): functions as noun — mercy as a state or quality. For *eleeō* (G1653, 28 active): verb — mercy as act. For *eleēmōn* (G1655, 2 active): adjective — mercy as character quality of a person. For *chanan* (H2603A, 72 active): verb (Qal and Piel) — to be gracious, to show grace; also forms person-type nouns. The full grammatical range (noun, verb, adjective, person-type noun) confirms mercy operates as condition (noun), action (verb), character (adjective), and identity (person-type). OBS-111-008, OBS-111-013. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (3 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-057 | OBSERVATION | Num 6:25 | Mercy engages perception in the seeing/facing dimension: Gen 32:20 — the goal of Jacob's mercy-seeking is "to see Esau's face" (OBS-111-040); Num 6:25 — "the Lord make his face shine upon you and be gracious to you" (shared anchor with R23); Rom 9:15 — mercy is tied to divine seeing (compassion). The mercy-seeing connection is relational perception — recognising the face of the merciful other. The *kopher*-bribe in 1Sa 12:3 ("blind my eyes") inverts mercy's perceptive engagement: the corrupted mercy-mechanism blinds the perceptive faculty. OBS-111-040, OBS-111-044. |
| OBS-111-T2-103 | OBSERVATION | Job 33:24 | The evidence contains one instance: Job 33:24 (an angel mediates and "is merciful to him, and says, 'Deliver him from going down into the pit; I have found a ransom'" — OBS-111-042). This names angelic mediation in the mercy-restoration sequence: an angel intercedes, God is merciful, a ransom is found. The angel participates in the mercy-event as intercessor. No other direct evidence of spiritual beings interface in R111's anchor verses. OBS-111-042. |
| OBS-111-T2-104 | OBSERVATION | Job 33:24 | One instance: Job 33:24 (angelic mediation in mercy-restoration — OBS-111-042). The angel's role appears to be intercessory — naming the ransom that enables mercy. The evidence does not develop this as a systematic pattern; it is a single instance that suggests angels can participate in mercy's mediating structure without being the source of mercy. OBS-111-042. |

**SD pointers — direct (1)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-023-SD021 | MEDIUM |  | 11 shared terms including supplication vocabulary (H8467 techinah, H8469 tahanun, H2603A chanan). The supplication vocabulary sits at the intersection of compassion and prayer: the inner act of pleading for mercy assumes that God is compassionate. The structural relationship: compassion in the recipient (God) grounds the act of supplication in the petitioner. Cross-registry: Pray (Reg 212), Prayer (Reg 122), Intercession (Reg 94). |
